choices.C: Government programs that distribute benefits based on income level are subject to more stringent fraud protections than programs with other eligibility criteria.
choices.D: A person is not morally culpable for misrepresenting information when the rules governing the distribution of a benefit are themselves unjust.
choices.E: The legal definition of fraud should be limited to cases in which the fraudulent party intends to cause harm to the agency administering the program.

explanation.correct: B matches the reasoning exactly. The argument concludes that the recipient commits fraud despite her belief that the criteria are unjust — the belief in injustice does not excuse the fraud. B articulates this as a general principle: submitting false information to obtain an unqualified benefit is fraud regardless of one's views about the fairness of the criteria.
explanation.A: A introduces a repayment obligation that the argument does not address. The argument is about whether fraud occurred, not about remedies.
explanation.C: C makes a comparative claim about income-based programs that the argument does not rely on.
explanation.D: D is the opposite of the argument's conclusion. The argument explicitly rejects D by stating that the fraud is fraud even if the criteria are believed to be unjust.
explanation.E: E introduces an intent-to-harm requirement that the argument does not rely on and that would narrow the definition of fraud in ways inconsistent with the argument.

questions.stimulus: Studies consistently show that in countries with strong social safety nets — generous unemployment benefits, universal healthcare, and robust public pensions — citizens report lower levels of life satisfaction than in countries with more limited social safety nets. This finding is surprising, since a strong safety net would seem to reduce economic anxiety and thus increase wellbeing.
questions.question_stem: Which one of the following, if true, most helps to explain the apparent discrepancy?

choices.A: Countries with strong social safety nets tend to have higher tax rates, which may reduce disposable income for middle-class workers.
choices.B: Life satisfaction surveys are conducted differently in different countries, making direct cross-country comparisons unreliable.
choices.C: Countries with strong social safety nets tend to have colder climates, and colder climates have been independently associated with lower reported life satisfaction.
choices.D: Citizens in countries with strong social safety nets are more likely to perceive their governments as inefficient and wasteful.
choices.E: The countries with limited social safety nets in which residents report high life satisfaction are generally wealthier on a per capita basis.

explanation.correct: E resolves the paradox by identifying a confounding variable. If the high-satisfaction, limited-safety-net countries are generally wealthier, then their citizens' high satisfaction may reflect their wealth rather than their limited safety net. And if the strong-safety-net countries happen to be less wealthy, their lower satisfaction may reflect economic conditions rather than the safety net itself. This explains how both facts can be true without implying the safety net reduces satisfaction.
explanation.A: A suggests a mechanism by which safety nets might reduce satisfaction (through taxes), which would partially explain the finding but is too speculative and only addresses one direction.
explanation.B: B challenges the reliability of the data rather than explaining the paradox. If the surveys are unreliable, there may be no real paradox to explain.
explanation.C: C offers climate as a confounding variable and is a legitimate resolution, but it relies on an assumption about which countries have cold climates that is not provided.
explanation.D: D proposes government perception as a mechanism but doesn't adequately explain the full paradox — why would strong safety nets specifically lead to perceptions of inefficiency that outweigh reduced anxiety?

questions.stimulus: A marine biology study found that coral reefs in heavily trafficked tourist areas are in significantly better condition than coral reefs in remote, undisturbed areas of the same ocean. This is puzzling, since tourist activities such as snorkeling and boat anchoring are known to damage coral.
questions.question_stem: Which one of the following, if true, most helps to resolve the apparent paradox?

choices.A: Tourists are generally more aware of environmental regulations than locals who fish in remote areas.
choices.B: Heavy tourist traffic in reef areas tends to generate revenue that funds active conservation and restoration efforts for those reefs.
choices.C: Snorkeling and diving in tourist areas is typically limited to designated zones that represent only a small fraction of the total reef area.
choices.D: Remote coral reefs are subject to warming ocean temperatures that damage coral, just as tourist reefs are.
choices.E: The tourist reefs in the study were selected because they were already known to be in good condition, creating a sampling bias.

explanation.correct: B resolves the paradox by showing why tourist traffic, despite causing direct damage, is associated with better reef conditions overall. The tourism revenue funds conservation and restoration that more than offsets the damage from tourist activities. This makes both facts simultaneously true: tourist activities damage coral AND tourist reefs are in better condition, because the funding they generate pays for active management.
explanation.A: A suggests tourists are more law-abiding but doesn't explain why tourist reefs would be in better condition than undisturbed remote reefs — awareness of regulations doesn't generate active restoration.
explanation.C: C explains how tourist damage is limited but doesn't explain why tourist reefs would be in better condition than completely undisturbed reefs.
explanation.D: D applies the same environmental stressor to both reef types, which does not help explain the discrepancy between them.
explanation.E: E suggests the study itself is flawed due to sampling bias. If true, this would dissolve the paradox by saying there is no real pattern to explain, rather than resolving the apparent contradiction.

questions.stimulus: A study of corporate executive compensation found that executives at companies that performed poorly over a five-year period received, on average, higher total compensation than executives at companies that performed well over the same period. This is surprising given that most executive compensation packages are structured to reward strong performance.
questions.question_stem: Which one of the following, if true, most helps to explain the paradox described above?

choices.A: Some companies that performed poorly during the period were in industries experiencing broad economic downturns beyond executives' control.
choices.B: Poorly performing companies often attempt to attract and retain talented executives by offering higher guaranteed salaries to compensate for the reduced value of performance-based incentives.
choices.C: The executives of well-performing companies frequently decline large bonuses in order to improve their companies' public image.
choices.D: Shareholders of poorly performing companies are more likely than those of well-performing companies to scrutinize executive pay packages.
choices.E: Performance-based compensation structures are more common in large companies than in small and medium-sized companies.

explanation.correct: B resolves the paradox. Poorly performing companies cannot attract executives with large performance bonuses (since performance is poor), so they offer higher guaranteed base salaries instead. This inflates total compensation despite poor performance. Well-performing companies can offer lower base salaries because their executives will receive large performance bonuses. Both facts are thus reconciled: structure differs, and total compensation of poorly-performing companies ends up higher.
explanation.A: A provides context for why companies performed poorly but does not explain why their executives would receive higher compensation than those at well-performing companies.
explanation.C: C proposes a behavior by well-performing executives (declining bonuses) that might partially explain lower total compensation, but the claim is speculative and doesn't address the structure of compensation at poorly performing companies.
explanation.D: D says shareholders at poor companies scrutinize pay more, which would suggest lower executive compensation at those companies — the opposite of the paradox.
explanation.E: E introduces a size variable that could relate to the finding but does not directly explain why poorly performing companies pay more.

questions.stimulus: Every student who passed Professor Vasquez's course submitted all required assignments. Marcus did not pass the course. Therefore, Marcus did not submit all required assignments.
questions.question_stem: The pattern of reasoning in which one of the following arguments is most similar to that in the argument above?

choices.A: Every vehicle that passed the annual emissions inspection meets federal pollution standards. This vehicle did not pass the inspection. Therefore, this vehicle does not meet federal pollution standards.
choices.B: Every vehicle that meets federal pollution standards passes the annual emissions inspection. This vehicle passed the inspection. Therefore, this vehicle meets federal pollution standards.
choices.C: Every vehicle that meets federal pollution standards passes the annual emissions inspection. This vehicle does not pass the inspection. Therefore, this vehicle does not meet federal pollution standards.
choices.D: Some vehicles that pass the annual emissions inspection do not meet federal pollution standards. This vehicle passed the inspection. Therefore, this vehicle may not meet federal pollution standards.
choices.E: Every vehicle that passes the annual emissions inspection meets federal pollution standards. This vehicle meets federal pollution standards. Therefore, this vehicle passed the annual emissions inspection.

explanation.correct: A matches the logical structure precisely. Original: All who passed → submitted all assignments; Marcus did not pass → Marcus did not submit all. This is the contrapositive: not all submitted → not passed. In A: All who passed inspection → meets standards; did not pass inspection → does not meet standards. Same structure: affirming the antecedent via contrapositive.
explanation.B: B reverses the conditional: it starts with 'meets standards → passes inspection' and then affirms the consequent (passed inspection) to conclude meets standards. This is affirming the consequent — a fallacy.
explanation.C: C is the valid contrapositive of a different conditional ('meets standards → passes inspection'): does not pass → does not meet standards. This is valid but the conditional is reversed from the original.
explanation.D: D introduces 'some' rather than 'all' and draws a probabilistic conclusion, which is a different logical structure.
explanation.E: E affirms the consequent of the original conditional: all who pass → meet standards; meets standards → therefore passed. Invalid inference.

questions.stimulus: The sports league argued that its television broadcast rights should not be subject to antitrust regulation because competition from other entertainment options — films, streaming services, live concerts — means that the league does not hold a monopoly position in the entertainment market. However, the league clearly holds a monopoly position in the market for professional football specifically, and it is that market that regulators are concerned with.
questions.question_stem: The reasoning in which one of the following is most similar to the reasoning in the argument above?

choices.A: A neighborhood association argued that a proposed coffee shop should not require a special use permit because there are already many restaurants in the neighborhood. However, there are no other coffee shops within walking distance, and the permit requirement applies to coffee shops specifically.
choices.B: A food manufacturer argued that its salt content did not exceed federal guidelines because the guidelines are set for daily total sodium intake, not for any single product. However, health advocates argued that the guidelines should be applied per serving regardless of daily totals.
choices.C: A company argued that its market share should not trigger antitrust review because the relevant market should be defined broadly to include all digital services. Regulators countered that the company's share of the narrower market for social networking specifically warranted review.
choices.D: A pharmaceutical company argued that its drug pricing was not anticompetitive because similar drugs existed on the market. Consumer groups countered that the similar drugs had significantly different side effect profiles and were not genuine alternatives.
choices.E: A restaurant chain argued that its hiring practices did not discriminate against older workers because its overall workforce age distribution matched regional averages. However, the discrimination complaint concerned only management-level hiring, where older workers were significantly underrepresented.

explanation.correct: C parallels the structure exactly. The league: 'not a monopoly in broad entertainment market' → regulators: 'you hold a monopoly in the specific market at issue.' C: company: 'not dominant in broad digital services market' → regulators: 'you dominate the specific social networking market at issue.' Both involve a party arguing for a broad market definition to escape regulation, and a regulator pointing to the relevant narrow market.
explanation.A: A involves a different type of regulatory process (use permits) and the parallel is imperfect — the coffee shop is arguing against a permit based on restaurant presence, not against antitrust review.
explanation.B: B involves a dispute about how a standard applies (daily vs. per-serving) rather than a dispute about the relevant market definition.
explanation.D: D involves a dispute about whether similar products are genuine substitutes — related but different from the market-definition dispute in the original.
explanation.E: E involves a dispute about the relevant employee category, which has a similar structure, but E is about employment discrimination rather than market power.

questions.stimulus:
Thornton: The university's decision to eliminate the philosophy department is short-sighted. Studying philosophy teaches critical thinking skills that are valuable across all disciplines and careers. Students who take philosophy courses consistently outperform those who don't on standardized tests of analytical reasoning.
Fowler: The university must make difficult budget decisions, and philosophy is one of the lowest-enrolled departments. Funds redirected from philosophy could support programs with much higher student demand, ultimately serving more students more effectively.
questions.question_stem: The dialogue most strongly suggests that Thornton and Fowler disagree about whether

choices.A: the university faces genuine budget constraints that require cuts to some programs
choices.B: the benefit of philosophy to enrolled students justifies maintaining the department given budget constraints
choices.C: students who study philosophy perform better on standardized tests of analytical reasoning
choices.D: philosophy is among the least-enrolled departments at the university
choices.E: redirecting funds from philosophy to high-demand programs would increase the overall quality of university education

explanation.correct: B captures the core disagreement. Thornton argues philosophy's benefits to students justify maintaining it; Fowler argues that budget constraints and low enrollment mean it does not serve students as well as alternatives would. Both have a clear position on this question: Thornton says yes, the benefits justify it; Fowler says no, they don't given the budget and enrollment realities.
explanation.A: A is not in dispute. Fowler explicitly acknowledges budget constraints, and Thornton does not appear to contest them.
explanation.C: C refers to Thornton's empirical claim about test scores, which Fowler does not address or dispute. We cannot determine Fowler's position on this specific claim.
explanation.D: D is factual background that Thornton does not appear to dispute.
explanation.E: E goes beyond what the dialogue establishes. Fowler claims more students would be served, not necessarily that quality would increase, and Thornton does not specifically address overall quality.

questions.stimulus:
Coordinator: The city's new composting program has been highly successful — participation rates have increased every year for three years, and the volume of food waste diverted from landfills has grown substantially.
Analyst: I wouldn't call it successful. The program's operational costs have exceeded its budget projections every year, and the city is now considering reducing the frequency of compost collection to cut costs. A program that cannot be sustained financially is not truly successful.
questions.question_stem: The point at issue between the coordinator and the analyst is

choices.A: whether the city's composting program should be continued despite its budget overruns
choices.B: whether participation rates and waste diversion are the appropriate measures by which to evaluate the composting program's success
choices.C: whether reducing the frequency of compost collection would cause participation rates to decline
choices.D: whether a public program that achieves its environmental goals but exceeds its budget constitutes a success
choices.E: whether the volume of food waste diverted from landfills has in fact grown substantially since the program began

explanation.correct: D captures the precise point of disagreement. The coordinator defines success in terms of participation and waste diversion goals — both of which have been met. The analyst defines success as requiring financial sustainability — a standard the program fails to meet. Both have a clear, opposed position on the question: the coordinator says yes (achieving environmental goals is success), the analyst says no (financial sustainability is required for genuine success).
explanation.A: A asks about whether the program should continue — neither speaker explicitly takes a position on this question. The coordinator says it has been successful, and the analyst says it hasn't, but neither makes a recommendation about continuation.
explanation.B: B is close but less precise. The real disagreement is not about which measures are appropriate in general but about whether the program, using any reasonable measures, qualifies as successful. D captures this more precisely.
explanation.C: C raises a consequence of a proposed change that neither speaker addresses.
explanation.E: E challenges a factual claim that the analyst does not explicitly dispute. The analyst accepts the waste diversion data but disputes whether it counts as success.

questions.stimulus:
Marcos: Mandatory military service instills discipline, civic responsibility, and a shared national identity across socioeconomic lines. Countries that have maintained mandatory service, such as South Korea and Israel, show strong social cohesion even amid political disagreements.
Leila: The social cohesion you observe in those countries likely stems from other historical and cultural factors rather than mandatory service. Moreover, mandatory service diverts young people from education and productive work at a critical stage of development, ultimately harming both individuals and the economy.
questions.question_stem: Marcos and Leila are committed to disagreeing about whether

choices.A: South Korea and Israel have strong social cohesion compared to countries without mandatory military service
choices.B: mandatory military service is a significant contributor to social cohesion in the countries that practice it
choices.C: the economic costs of mandatory military service outweigh any social benefits it might provide
choices.D: young people in countries with mandatory military service are harmed by being diverted from education and productive work
choices.E: countries that have abolished mandatory military service have experienced declines in social cohesion

explanation.correct: B identifies the specific causal claim they disagree about. Marcos argues mandatory service produces social cohesion; Leila argues the cohesion in those countries comes from other factors — directly denying that mandatory service is responsible. Both have clear, opposed positions: Marcos yes, Leila no.
explanation.A: A is a factual claim that Leila appears to accept — she attributes the cohesion to other factors rather than denying the cohesion exists. Both speakers may agree that South Korea and Israel have social cohesion.
explanation.C: C requires weighing costs against benefits. Leila argues about economic costs, but Marcos does not directly engage with this trade-off. We cannot be sure Marcos has a stated position on this comparative weighing.
explanation.D: D is Leila's claim that Marcos does not explicitly address. We don't know Marcos's position on the economic diversion question.
explanation.E: E raises a counterfactual about countries that abolished mandatory service that neither speaker directly addresses.

questions.stimulus: A recent survey found that students who reported eating breakfast daily scored higher on standardized tests than students who did not. The school board concluded that requiring all students to eat breakfast before school would improve test scores across the district.
questions.question_stem: The school board's reasoning is flawed because it

choices.A: relies on a survey that may not be representative of the district's student population
choices.B: assumes that a correlation between two factors establishes that one causes the other
choices.C: overlooks the possibility that some students have dietary restrictions
choices.D: fails to consider whether improved test scores are a meaningful educational goal
choices.E: presupposes that mandatory policies are more effective than voluntary ones

explanation.correct: B is correct. The argument moves from the observation that breakfast-eating and higher scores co-occur to the conclusion that breakfast causes higher scores. This is a classic correlation-causation flaw — it's equally possible that a third factor (e.g., socioeconomic stability) causes both.
explanation.A: A is incorrect. While survey representativeness is a legitimate concern, the core flaw is the causal inference from correlational data, not sampling methodology.
explanation.B: See correct explanation above.
explanation.C: C is incorrect. Dietary restrictions are a practical implementation concern, not the logical flaw in the reasoning.
explanation.D: D is incorrect. The argument doesn't question whether test scores are valuable — it assumes they are, which is reasonable. This isn't the flaw.
explanation.E: E is incorrect. The argument doesn't compare mandatory vs. voluntary policies; it simply recommends a mandatory policy, which is a conclusion, not a flawed premise.

questions.stimulus: No legislation that increases regulatory burdens on small businesses has ever passed in this state without the support of the Chamber of Commerce. The proposed environmental disclosure bill increases regulatory burdens on small businesses. Therefore, the Chamber of Commerce must support the environmental disclosure bill.
questions.question_stem: The argument above is vulnerable to criticism because it

choices.A: treats a necessary condition as though it were a sufficient condition
choices.B: treats a sufficient condition as though it were a necessary condition
choices.C: draws a conclusion about future events based solely on past patterns
choices.D: fails to consider whether the Chamber of Commerce has changed its policy positions
choices.E: relies on ambiguous use of the phrase 'regulatory burdens'

explanation.correct: A is correct. The original statement establishes that Chamber support is necessary for such legislation to pass (it has never passed without that support). The argument then concludes that Chamber support is present — treating the necessary condition as though Chamber support is guaranteed whenever the conditions apply. The logic would only work if Chamber support were sufficient, not merely necessary.
explanation.A: See correct explanation above.
explanation.B: B is incorrect. The argument doesn't treat a sufficient condition as necessary; rather it treats a necessary condition as sufficient.
explanation.C: C is incorrect. While the argument does rely on past patterns, this isn't the specific logical flaw — the core error is the necessary/sufficient confusion.
explanation.D: D is incorrect. Whether the Chamber changed positions is a practical concern, not the logical structure flaw.
explanation.E: E is incorrect. The phrase 'regulatory burdens' is used consistently and the argument's flaw is structural, not semantic.

questions.stimulus: Every pilot who has flown over 10,000 hours has reported experiencing mild cognitive fatigue during extended missions. Since Captain Reyes has flown over 10,000 hours, she must experience mild cognitive fatigue during extended missions.
questions.question_stem: The reasoning in the argument is flawed because it

choices.A: assumes that self-reported data accurately reflects actual cognitive states
choices.B: applies a generalization about a group to a specific member of that group without sufficient justification
choices.C: concludes that all pilots are at risk based on data from a subset of pilots
choices.D: overlooks the possibility that Captain Reyes takes countermeasures against fatigue
choices.E: confuses a correlation between flight hours and fatigue with a causal relationship

explanation.correct: B is correct. The argument takes a statistical generalization — every pilot surveyed reported X — and applies it as a certainty to a specific individual. Even if the generalization holds broadly, it doesn't logically guarantee it applies to any single case.
explanation.A: A is incorrect. The accuracy of self-reporting is not the structural issue here; the problem is applying group-level data to an individual.
explanation.B: See correct explanation above.
explanation.C: C is incorrect. The argument doesn't generalize about all pilots — it applies a claim about all pilots to one specific pilot.
explanation.D: D is incorrect. Countermeasures are a practical consideration and don't address the logical flaw in the inference.
explanation.E: E is incorrect. The stimulus presents the relationship as universal ('every pilot...'), so the flaw isn't correlation vs. causation — it's the application of a group claim to an individual.

questions.stimulus: Professor Tanaka argues that the university should eliminate its philosophy department because philosophy graduates have lower average starting salaries than graduates from other departments. But Professor Tanaka's own daughter is a pre-law student who relies on philosophy coursework for her LSAT preparation. Therefore, we should not trust his argument.
questions.question_stem: The reasoning above is flawed because it

choices.A: attacks Professor Tanaka's personal character rather than addressing his argument
choices.B: draws attention to a conflict of interest that may bias Professor Tanaka's conclusion
choices.C: assumes that personal relationships affect the validity of economic arguments
choices.D: dismisses a claim solely on the basis of the claimant's circumstances rather than engaging with the substance of the argument
choices.E: uses the professor's conclusion to discredit his premises

explanation.correct: D is correct. This is an ad hominem circumstantial flaw. The argument dismisses the professor's position by pointing to his family circumstances rather than addressing whether his argument — that philosophy graduates earn less — is actually correct. The professor's daughter's situation is irrelevant to the logical force of his evidence.
explanation.A: A is incorrect. The argument doesn't attack Professor Tanaka's character (e.g., calling him dishonest or incompetent) — it points to a circumstance. The flaw is circumstantial ad hominem, not personal attack.
explanation.B: B describes what the argument is trying to do, but it doesn't identify why doing so is flawed. A conflict of interest can be noted but still doesn't refute the argument.
explanation.C: C is too vague and doesn't identify the specific logical error.
explanation.D: See correct explanation above.
explanation.E: E is incorrect. The argument doesn't use the conclusion against the premises — it uses personal circumstances to reject the entire argument.

questions.stimulus: Either the city must raise property taxes or it must cut public transit funding. The mayor has publicly opposed any property tax increases. Therefore, public transit funding will be cut.
questions.question_stem: The argument above is flawed because it

choices.A: treats the mayor's public statements as a definitive policy commitment
choices.B: assumes without justification that there are no other available options beyond the two presented
choices.C: fails to explain why cutting transit funding is preferable to raising taxes
choices.D: draws a conclusion about future policy without considering the views of the city council
choices.E: relies on a conditional claim without establishing that its antecedent is true

explanation.correct: B is correct. This is a false dichotomy. The argument presents only two options (raise property taxes or cut transit funding), but there may be other alternatives: cutting different programs, issuing bonds, applying for federal grants, etc. The either/or framing is not justified.
explanation.A: A is incorrect. While mayoral promises can be broken, the more fundamental flaw is that the two-option framing itself may be false.
explanation.B: See correct explanation above.
explanation.C: C is incorrect. The argument doesn't need to explain which option is preferable — it concludes which will happen. This isn't the logical flaw.
explanation.D: D is incorrect. The city council's views are relevant to predicting outcomes but not to the logical structure of the argument.
explanation.E: E is incorrect. The argument's structure isn't a conditional — it's a disjunction (either/or), and the flaw is in that disjunction being presented as exhaustive.

questions.stimulus: Studies show that neighborhoods with more coffee shops have lower crime rates. City planners have therefore proposed subsidizing the opening of coffee shops in high-crime areas as a strategy for reducing crime.
questions.question_stem: The planners' proposal is based on flawed reasoning because it

choices.A: misidentifies the direction of a causal relationship by assuming that coffee shops reduce crime rather than that low-crime areas attract coffee shops
choices.B: fails to establish that subsidizing businesses is a legitimate use of public funds
choices.C: relies on neighborhood-level data that may not apply to individual blocks within those neighborhoods
choices.D: overlooks the possibility that coffee shop patrons may themselves engage in criminal activity
choices.E: assumes that correlation between two variables cannot exist without a causal mechanism

explanation.correct: A is correct. The argument identifies a correlation but misassigns the causal direction. It's far more plausible that low crime rates and economic stability attract coffee shops (businesses seek safe, affluent areas) rather than that coffee shops cause reductions in crime. The planners have reversed the likely causal arrow.
explanation.A: See correct explanation above.
explanation.B: B is incorrect. The legitimacy of public subsidies is a normative question irrelevant to the logical flaw in the causal reasoning.
explanation.C: C is incorrect. Granularity of data is a methodological concern, not the core logical error.
explanation.D: D is incorrect. This is speculative and doesn't address the structural flaw in causal reasoning.
explanation.E: E is incorrect and misrepresents what the argument assumes. The argument doesn't question whether correlations require causal mechanisms — it just picks the wrong causal direction.

questions.stimulus: Dr. Okonkwo's new book argues that traditional herbal remedies are more effective than pharmaceutical drugs for treating chronic pain. However, Dr. Okonkwo receives substantial funding from a herbal supplement company. His conclusions should therefore be dismissed.
questions.question_stem: The argument is vulnerable to criticism because it

choices.A: fails to specify which traditional herbal remedies are being compared to which pharmaceutical drugs
choices.B: rejects a conclusion on the basis of the source's potential bias rather than by identifying errors in the reasoning or evidence
choices.C: assumes that financial conflicts of interest always produce inaccurate research results
choices.D: confuses the motivation for conducting research with the validity of the research methodology
choices.E: both B and C

explanation.correct: B is correct. The argument commits the genetic fallacy — dismissing a claim based on its origin (who made it and why) rather than evaluating the actual evidence or reasoning. Even if Dr. Okonkwo has a financial motive, his conclusions could still be correct. The argument gives no reason to think the data is wrong.
explanation.A: A is incorrect. While specificity would strengthen the argument, the absence of specifics is not the flaw identified here.
explanation.B: See correct explanation above.
explanation.C: C is a possible implicit assumption but isn't the identified flaw — the flaw is the dismissal itself without engaging the argument.
explanation.D: D is close but less precise. The core error is rejecting the conclusion based on origin, not specifically confusing motivation with methodology.
explanation.E: E is incorrect. Only B precisely identifies the logical flaw.

questions.stimulus: The ancient Greeks had no concept of zero as a number, and yet they produced extraordinary advances in geometry and philosophy. Therefore, the modern emphasis on teaching number theory in early childhood is misguided — children can clearly develop mathematical thinking without it.
questions.question_stem: The argument above is most vulnerable to the criticism that it

choices.A: relies on an analogy between ancient scholars and modern children that ignores relevant differences
choices.B: assumes that mathematical achievements in one domain demonstrate competency across all mathematical domains
choices.C: draws a conclusion about educational policy from evidence about historical intellectual accomplishment
choices.D: overlooks the possibility that exposure to number theory may benefit children even if it is not strictly necessary
choices.E: treats an exceptional historical case as evidence about what is typical or standard

explanation.correct: A is correct. The argument compares ancient Greek scholars — adults operating in a specialized intellectual tradition — to modern children in early education. These groups are so different in context, purpose, and development that the analogy breaks down. The absence of zero from Greek mathematics doesn't tell us much about what concepts young children need to build foundational numeracy.
explanation.A: See correct explanation above.
explanation.B: B is incorrect. The argument doesn't claim the Greeks were competent in all mathematical domains — it uses their achievements as evidence for an educational claim.
explanation.C: C describes what the argument does but doesn't identify why that inference fails. The flaw is specifically the disanalogy.
explanation.D: D identifies a gap in the argument but not the core logical flaw in the comparison.
explanation.E: E is partially relevant but less precise — the Greeks weren't just exceptional individuals but an entire civilization, and the issue is the false analogy rather than treating outliers as typical.

questions.stimulus: This pharmaceutical company has invested more in cancer research over the last decade than any other private institution. It follows that this company is more committed to improving patient health than any other private institution.
questions.question_stem: The argument's reasoning is flawed because it

choices.A: assumes that financial investment is the only meaningful measure of institutional commitment
choices.B: fails to consider that other institutions may have invested equally in other disease areas
choices.C: conflates research investment with the outcomes that investment produces
choices.D: presupposes that cancer research is the most important form of medical research
choices.E: ignores the possibility that the company invests heavily for financial rather than altruistic reasons

explanation.correct: E is correct. The argument infers genuine commitment to patient health from high investment, but investment can be driven by profit motive — cancer treatments are a multi-billion dollar market. High investment is consistent with zero genuine commitment to patient health. The argument assumes investment reflects commitment without ruling out commercial motivations.
explanation.A: A is partially relevant — financial investment may not be the only measure — but E more precisely names the flaw: even if investment is the right measure, its motivation undermines what it supposedly measures.
explanation.B: B is incorrect. The argument is about this company's commitment, not a comparison to others' investments in other areas.
explanation.C: C is incorrect. The argument doesn't conflate investment with outcomes — it conflates investment with commitment, which is a different error.
explanation.D: D is incorrect. The argument doesn't claim cancer research is the most important area — it uses that investment as evidence of broader commitment.
explanation.E: See correct explanation above.

questions.stimulus: A study of 500 adults found that those who described themselves as optimistic lived, on average, seven years longer than those who described themselves as pessimistic. Researchers concluded that optimism directly extends human lifespan.
questions.question_stem: The researchers' conclusion is most vulnerable to which of the following criticisms?

choices.A: The study relied on self-reported data, which may not accurately reflect participants' actual psychological states
choices.B: The study did not control for the possibility that underlying health conditions cause both pessimism and shorter lifespans
choices.C: A sample of 500 adults is too small to support conclusions about the general population
choices.D: The study failed to distinguish between optimism as a trait and optimism as a temporary mood
choices.E: Lifespan differences of seven years may not be practically significant in clinical settings

explanation.correct: B is correct. This identifies the third variable problem. A confounding variable — chronic illness — could independently cause both pessimism (sick people feel worse about the future) and shorter lifespans. Without controlling for health status, the correlation between optimism and longevity may be entirely spurious. The researchers' causal conclusion is not supported.
explanation.A: A is a legitimate methodological concern but not the most serious flaw. Even if self-reports are somewhat inaccurate, the fundamental issue is confounding, not measurement error.
explanation.B: See correct explanation above.
explanation.C: C is incorrect. 500 participants is a reasonable sample size for many studies. This isn't the most serious flaw.
explanation.D: D is incorrect. The optimism/trait distinction is a theoretical nuance that doesn't undermine the core causal inference.
explanation.E: E is incorrect. Clinical significance is separate from whether the researchers' causal conclusion is logically supported.

questions.stimulus: Urban green spaces provide measurable mental health benefits to nearby residents. However, maintaining these spaces is expensive, and many cities are facing budget shortfalls. Some officials argue that parks should be privatized to reduce costs. But privatization historically results in restricted public access, which defeats the purpose of having public green spaces at all.
questions.question_stem: The main conclusion of the argument is that

choices.A: urban green spaces provide mental health benefits to nearby residents
choices.B: cities are facing budget shortfalls that threaten park maintenance
choices.C: privatizing parks would restrict access, undermining the value of public green spaces
choices.D: private management of parks is always less effective than public management
choices.E: mental health benefits are the primary reason cities should maintain public parks

explanation.correct: C is correct. The argument's logical destination is the claim that privatization would undermine the purpose of public green spaces. The mental health benefits and budget constraints are background premises; the officials' proposal is what the argument pushes back against, and the conclusion is that privatization defeats its own purpose.
explanation.A: A is a supporting premise, not the conclusion. It establishes why parks matter.
explanation.B: B is another premise — it sets up the problem that leads to the privatization proposal.
explanation.C: See correct explanation above.
explanation.D: D is too broad and not supported. The argument makes a specific point about access, not a general claim about management effectiveness.
explanation.E: E is incorrect. The argument doesn't claim mental health is the primary reason — just one important reason.

questions.stimulus: Mandatory minimum sentencing laws were introduced to reduce judicial discretion and ensure uniform punishment for similar crimes. Yet studies consistently show that these laws have not reduced crime rates. Moreover, they have led to prison overcrowding and have disproportionately affected low-income communities. We must therefore reconsider whether mandatory minimums serve the goals they were designed to achieve.
questions.question_stem: Which of the following best states the main conclusion of the argument?

choices.A: Mandatory minimum sentencing has led to prison overcrowding and racial disparities
choices.B: Judicial discretion in sentencing should be fully restored
choices.C: The effectiveness and appropriateness of mandatory minimum sentencing laws should be reexamined
choices.D: Mandatory minimum laws have failed to reduce crime rates in the jurisdictions that enacted them
choices.E: Criminal sentencing policy should prioritize rehabilitation over uniform punishment

explanation.correct: C is correct. The argument's conclusion is explicitly signaled by 'We must therefore' — it's a call to reconsider whether mandatory minimums achieve their intended goals. The evidence (no crime reduction, overcrowding, disparate impact) supports this recommendation.
explanation.A: A is a premise — one piece of evidence in the argument.
explanation.B: B goes beyond what the argument concludes. 'Reconsider' is weaker than 'fully restore' — the argument doesn't advocate for a specific replacement policy.
explanation.C: See correct explanation above.
explanation.D: D is one of the premises (the crime rate finding), not the conclusion.
explanation.E: E introduces a specific policy direction (rehabilitation) that the argument never mentions.

questions.stimulus: Insects are an efficient source of protein, requiring far less land, water, and feed than conventional livestock. Several countries in Asia and Africa have incorporated insects into their diets for centuries with no adverse health effects. Western consumers' reluctance to eat insects is therefore based entirely on cultural bias rather than legitimate health or environmental concerns.
questions.question_stem: The main conclusion drawn in the argument is that

choices.A: insects are more environmentally sustainable than conventional livestock
choices.B: Western consumers should be required to incorporate insects into their diets
choices.C: Western resistance to eating insects is rooted in cultural prejudice, not health or environmental grounds
choices.D: insects have been safely consumed for centuries in many parts of the world
choices.E: food cultures that include insects are more rational than those that do not

explanation.correct: C is correct. The word 'therefore' signals the conclusion. The argument uses the efficiency and safety evidence to conclude that Western objections have no substantive basis — they're purely cultural.
explanation.A: A is a premise — evidence used to support the conclusion.
explanation.B: B is not stated in the argument. The argument criticizes Western resistance but doesn't advocate for mandates.
explanation.C: See correct explanation above.
explanation.D: D is a premise about historical consumption patterns.
explanation.E: E is a value judgment that goes further than what the argument claims.

questions.stimulus: Those who claim that economic growth always benefits the poorest members of society point to rising average incomes during periods of expansion. But averages can be misleading when wealth is distributed unequally. Indeed, in recent growth periods, median household income has stagnated even as average income has risen sharply. A rising average, therefore, tells us little about whether economic conditions are improving for typical households.
questions.question_stem: The conclusion of the argument is best expressed as which of the following?

choices.A: Economic growth does not benefit the poorest members of society
choices.B: Median household income is a better measure of economic wellbeing than average income
choices.C: Average income increases do not reliably indicate improvements in economic conditions for most households
choices.D: Wealth inequality has increased during recent periods of economic growth
choices.E: Claims about economic growth benefiting all members of society are deliberately misleading

explanation.correct: C is correct. The argument's conclusion — signaled by 'therefore' — is that a rising average income fails to tell us whether typical households are better off. This is a measured epistemological point about the limits of averages as evidence.
explanation.A: A is too strong. The argument says averages are uninformative, not that growth definitely fails the poor.
explanation.B: B is implied but not stated as the conclusion. The argument uses the median/average contrast as evidence, not as the conclusion.
explanation.C: See correct explanation above.
explanation.D: D is a factual claim used as evidence (median stagnating while average rises implies inequality), not the argument's conclusion.
explanation.E: E attributes intent (deliberate deception) that the argument never claims.

questions.stimulus: Antibiotics are effective only against bacterial infections, not viral ones. Yet many patients demand antibiotics when they have colds or flu — both viral illnesses. Prescribing antibiotics in these cases does not help patients recover faster. Worse, overprescription contributes to antibiotic resistance, making these drugs less effective for everyone. Physicians should therefore decline to prescribe antibiotics when patients present with viral infections.
questions.question_stem: The main point of the argument is that

choices.A: antibiotics are ineffective against viruses
choices.B: antibiotic resistance is a serious public health threat caused by overprescription
choices.C: doctors should refuse to prescribe antibiotics to patients with viral infections
choices.D: patients often misunderstand which conditions antibiotics can treat
choices.E: the medical community should take stronger action against antibiotic overprescription

explanation.correct: C is correct. The conclusion is signaled by 'therefore' and directly states what physicians should do. All prior claims — ineffectiveness against viruses, patient demands, resistance — are premises supporting this recommendation.
explanation.A: A is a premise — it establishes the factual basis for the argument.
explanation.B: B is another premise — the harm caused by overprescription.
explanation.C: See correct explanation above.
explanation.D: D is implied but not stated as the conclusion.
explanation.E: E is broader than the argument's conclusion, which addresses individual physician behavior, not systemic policy.

questions.stimulus: Scientific consensus on climate change is often portrayed in the media as more contested than it actually is among researchers. This false balance — giving equal coverage to fringe views and mainstream scientific opinion — misleads the public about the degree of expert agreement. Informed democratic deliberation on climate policy requires accurate understanding of where scientific agreement lies.
questions.question_stem: Which of the following most accurately expresses the main conclusion of this argument?

choices.A: The media routinely misrepresents the level of scientific agreement on climate change
choices.B: Fringe scientific views should not be covered by mainstream media outlets
choices.C: Democratic policy-making depends on the public having accurate information about scientific consensus
choices.D: Media coverage of climate science undermines the public's ability to make informed judgments about climate policy
choices.E: Scientific consensus on climate change is stronger than most people believe

explanation.correct: D is correct. The argument moves through several claims: media distorts consensus → public is misled → informed deliberation requires accurate understanding. The logical endpoint is that media distortion undermines informed democratic deliberation on climate policy — which is what D captures.
explanation.A: A is a premise — it describes what the media does wrong.
explanation.B: B is a prescription the argument doesn't actually make. It critiques false balance but doesn't say fringe views should never be covered.
explanation.C: C is also a premise — a supporting claim about what good deliberation requires.
explanation.D: See correct explanation above.
explanation.E: E is background context, not the conclusion.

questions.stimulus: Historical preservation advocates argue that demolishing old buildings erases cultural memory. Developers respond that these buildings are inefficient and costly to maintain. Both sides miss the key point: the choice between demolition and full preservation is a false one. Adaptive reuse — converting historic structures for contemporary purposes — preserves cultural memory while meeting modern needs.
questions.question_stem: The main conclusion of the argument is that

choices.A: historical preservation advocates and developers are both wrong
choices.B: adaptive reuse is a superior approach to historic buildings because it achieves both preservation and practical utility
choices.C: the debate between preservation and demolition misses an important alternative
choices.D: demolishing historic buildings is never justified when adaptive reuse is possible
choices.E: old buildings can be expensive and inefficient to maintain

explanation.correct: B is correct. While the argument does say both sides miss the point (C), that observation is in service of advocating for adaptive reuse as the better solution. The full conclusion is that adaptive reuse is superior because it addresses both concerns — preservation and modernity.
explanation.A: A is too dismissive. The argument doesn't say both sides are simply 'wrong' — it says they're working with a false dichotomy.
explanation.B: See correct explanation above.
explanation.C: C captures part of the conclusion but stops short. The argument goes further to advocate for a specific alternative.
explanation.D: D is too absolute — the argument doesn't say demolition is never justified.
explanation.E: E is a premise drawn from the developers' position.

questions.stimulus: Many critics contend that social media platforms should be legally liable for the content their users post, just as publishers are liable for what they publish. But social media platforms host billions of posts per day — a volume no editorial staff could review. Holding platforms liable under a publisher model would therefore either require impossibly large moderation teams or, more likely, force platforms to restrict user expression so severely that the open internet as we know it would cease to exist.
questions.question_stem: The main conclusion of the argument is that

choices.A: social media platforms already moderate content more than critics acknowledge
choices.B: applying publisher-style liability to social media would have unacceptable consequences for free expression
choices.C: social media companies cannot feasibly review all content posted on their platforms
choices.D: the open internet is a public good that should be protected by law
choices.E: critics of social media platforms misunderstand how internet content moderation works

explanation.correct: B is correct. The argument acknowledges the critics' position, raises the scale problem as a premise, and concludes (signaled by 'therefore') that publisher-style liability would either be operationally impossible or severely curtail free expression — an unacceptable outcome.
explanation.A: A is not mentioned in the argument.
explanation.B: See correct explanation above.
explanation.C: C is a premise that supports the conclusion but isn't the conclusion itself.
explanation.D: D is not a claim the argument makes.
explanation.E: E is implied but too vague — the argument makes a specific consequentialist point, not a general claim about misunderstanding.

questions.stimulus: Languages that lack a word for a particular color may cause speakers to perceive that color differently in certain tasks. This suggests that the vocabulary of a language shapes how its speakers perceive the world, not merely how they describe it.
questions.question_stem: The argument's main conclusion is that

choices.A: languages with richer color vocabularies produce speakers with more acute visual perception
choices.B: language does more than describe reality — it actively shapes perception
choices.C: speakers of different languages perceive color differently
choices.D: the vocabulary of a language reflects the perceptual priorities of its speakers' culture
choices.E: linguistic relativity is fully confirmed by color perception research

explanation.correct: B is correct. The argument's conclusion is precisely stated in the final sentence: that vocabulary shapes perception, not just description. The color example is the evidence.
explanation.A: A is too specific and not stated — the argument doesn't say richer vocabulary produces more acute perception generally.
explanation.B: See correct explanation above.
explanation.C: C is the evidence (the finding), not the conclusion.
explanation.D: D introduces a cultural reflection idea not present in the argument.
explanation.E: E is too strong — the argument says the evidence 'suggests,' not 'confirms.'

questions.stimulus: A company's legal obligation to maximize shareholder value is often cited as the reason corporations cannot prioritize environmental or social goals. But this obligation is routinely mischaracterized. Courts have consistently held that directors have broad discretion in determining what serves long-term shareholder interests, and sustainable practices frequently do serve long-term interests by reducing regulatory risk and building brand loyalty. The shareholder value doctrine, properly understood, does not prohibit socially responsible corporate behavior.
questions.question_stem: Which of the following best captures the main conclusion of the argument?

choices.A: Companies that adopt sustainable practices tend to be more profitable in the long run
choices.B: The legal doctrine of shareholder primacy has been misinterpreted as blocking corporate social responsibility
choices.C: Courts have given corporate directors wide latitude in interpreting their fiduciary duties
choices.D: The shareholder value doctrine, correctly interpreted, is compatible with socially responsible business conduct
choices.E: Environmental and social goals are increasingly aligned with shareholder financial interests

explanation.correct: D is correct. The argument works toward the final claim: the shareholder value doctrine 'properly understood' does not prohibit socially responsible behavior. All other statements are steps toward this conclusion.
explanation.A: A is implied but is a premise (sustainable practices serve long-term interests), not the final conclusion.
explanation.B: B is a near-conclusion sub-claim — it describes the mischaracterization — but the argument goes further to say what the correct characterization is.
explanation.C: C is a premise (court precedent) used to support the conclusion.
explanation.D: See correct explanation above.
explanation.E: E is a premise about how sustainability serves shareholder interests, not the concluding claim.

questions.stimulus: Councilwoman Diaz argues that the city should ban single-use plastic bags because they harm marine wildlife. Councilman Roth responds that many residents, particularly elderly and disabled residents, rely on these bags for disposing of medical waste, and that an outright ban without alternative provisions would create a hardship for vulnerable populations.
questions.question_stem: Councilman Roth responds to Councilwoman Diaz's argument by

choices.A: questioning whether single-use plastic bags actually harm marine wildlife
choices.B: arguing that the environmental benefits of the ban are overstated
choices.C: introducing a consideration that complicates the case for an unqualified ban
choices.D: proposing a compromise policy that would achieve both goals simultaneously
choices.E: challenging the factual premises on which Diaz's argument rests

explanation.correct: C is correct. Roth doesn't deny Diaz's environmental premise. Instead, he introduces a countervailing consideration — the hardship for vulnerable populations — that complicates the simple case for an outright ban. He's not proposing a compromise (he offers no alternative), just identifying a cost the original argument overlooks.
explanation.A: A is incorrect. Roth never disputes whether bags harm wildlife.
explanation.B: B is incorrect. Roth doesn't address the magnitude of environmental benefits.
explanation.C: See correct explanation above.
explanation.D: D is incorrect. Roth doesn't propose an alternative policy — he only raises an objection.
explanation.E: E is incorrect. Roth accepts Diaz's environmental premise and raises a different concern.

questions.stimulus: Proponents of the new transit plan argue that it will reduce commute times. But similar transit plans implemented in comparable cities over the past decade have consistently led to increased commute times during a multi-year adjustment period before benefits materialized. The plan's proponents are therefore overstating the near-term benefits.
questions.question_stem: The argument proceeds by

choices.A: providing evidence that the transit plan will ultimately fail to reduce commute times
choices.B: using analogous cases to challenge the accuracy of a specific prediction
choices.C: arguing that the transit plan's costs outweigh its benefits
choices.D: questioning the expertise of those who designed the transit plan
choices.E: demonstrating that the proponents of the plan have a conflict of interest

explanation.correct: B is correct. The argument uses comparisons to similar transit implementations in comparable cities to argue that the near-term benefits are overstated. It reasons from analogous cases to cast doubt on a specific prediction about near-term commute times.
explanation.A: A is incorrect. The argument says there will be a delay before benefits materialize — not that the plan will ultimately fail.
explanation.B: See correct explanation above.
explanation.C: C is incorrect. The argument doesn't discuss costs at all.
explanation.D: D is incorrect. No reference to the planners' expertise is made.
explanation.E: E is incorrect. No conflict of interest is alleged.

questions.stimulus:
Activist: The factory farm industry should be abolished because the conditions animals are kept in constitute unnecessary suffering.
Industry spokesperson: The activist eats processed food that contains additives produced through animal testing. It is hypocritical to oppose animal suffering while benefiting from it.
questions.question_stem: The industry spokesperson responds to the activist's argument by

choices.A: demonstrating that the activist's conclusion is factually incorrect
choices.B: arguing that animal suffering in agriculture is in fact necessary
choices.C: attacking the consistency of the activist's personal behavior rather than addressing the argument's merit
choices.D: providing an alternative account of what constitutes unnecessary suffering
choices.E: questioning whether the activist's proposed policy would actually reduce animal suffering

explanation.correct: C is correct. The spokesperson uses a tu quoque (you too) strategy — pointing to inconsistency in the activist's personal behavior rather than engaging with whether factory farming actually constitutes unnecessary suffering. Even if the activist is hypocritical, that doesn't address whether the argument is correct.
explanation.A: A is incorrect. The spokesperson doesn't address whether factory farm conditions cause unnecessary suffering.
explanation.B: B is incorrect. The spokesperson doesn't argue necessity — that would actually engage the argument.
explanation.C: See correct explanation above.
explanation.D: D is incorrect. No alternative definition of suffering is offered.
explanation.E: E is incorrect. Policy effectiveness is not addressed.

questions.stimulus: The administration claims that its new grading policy will reduce grade inflation. However, grade inflation has also declined at institutions that have not adopted this policy. Therefore, the policy cannot be given credit for any reductions in grade inflation that occur.
questions.question_stem: The argument uses which of the following reasoning strategies?

choices.A: It identifies an alternative explanation that undermines the administration's causal claim
choices.B: It argues that grade inflation is not a genuine problem worth addressing
choices.C: It provides data showing that the policy has already failed to reduce grade inflation
choices.D: It accepts the administration's conclusion but disputes the reasoning used to reach it
choices.E: It shows that the administration's policy produces unintended negative consequences

explanation.correct: A is correct. The argument points out that grade inflation has declined even without the policy, suggesting an alternative explanation — perhaps a broader trend — for any decline that occurs. This undermines the administration's implied causal claim that the policy is responsible for reductions.
explanation.A: See correct explanation above.
explanation.B: B is incorrect. The argument doesn't question whether grade inflation matters.
explanation.C: C is incorrect. The argument doesn't say the policy has already failed — it says any success can't be credited to the policy.
explanation.D: D is incorrect. The argument disputes the administration's causal attribution, not just the reasoning.
explanation.E: E is incorrect. No negative consequences are identified.

questions.stimulus: Some economists argue that rent control reduces housing quality because landlords lose the incentive to maintain properties when profits are capped. But this argument applies equally to regulated utilities — electricity and water providers operate under similar profit constraints and yet maintain high service quality, partly because regulators impose minimum maintenance standards. The economists' argument therefore proves too much.
questions.question_stem: The argument counters the economists' position by

choices.A: providing empirical evidence that rent-controlled apartments are better maintained than uncontrolled ones
choices.B: showing that the economists' reasoning, if valid, would lead to an implausible conclusion about regulated utilities
choices.C: demonstrating that regulators can successfully impose maintenance standards on rent-controlled landlords
choices.D: arguing that housing quality depends primarily on regulatory enforcement rather than landlord incentives
choices.E: questioning whether rent control actually reduces landlord profit margins

explanation.correct: B is correct. The argument uses a form of reductio ad absurdum: if the economists' reasoning (profit caps reduce quality) were valid, it would apply equally to regulated utilities — but utilities maintain quality under similar constraints, so the economists' premise doesn't hold as broadly as claimed. The argument 'proves too much' means it implies something the economists wouldn't accept.
explanation.A: A is incorrect. No empirical data about apartment maintenance quality is provided.
explanation.B: See correct explanation above.
explanation.C: C is closer but misses the structure — the argument isn't primarily recommending regulation; it's using utilities as a counterexample to the economists' logic.
explanation.D: D is a possible implication but not what the argument actually argues.
explanation.E: E is incorrect. The argument accepts the profit cap premise and challenges the inference from it.

questions.stimulus: Editorial: The senator claims her tax proposal will stimulate economic growth. She reaches this conclusion by citing two economists who support it. But hundreds of economists in the same field oppose it. The senator is selectively citing evidence to support a predetermined conclusion.
questions.question_stem: The editorial's argument proceeds by

choices.A: demonstrating that the senator's two economists are less qualified than the opposing majority
choices.B: arguing that economic policy should always follow majority expert opinion
choices.C: accusing the senator of cherry-picking favorable evidence while ignoring the weight of contrary expert opinion
choices.D: providing an alternative economic theory that better explains current growth trends
choices.E: questioning whether tax policy can stimulate economic growth in the first place

explanation.correct: C is correct. The editorial argues that the senator is engaging in selective evidence — citing two supporters while ignoring the much larger body of opposing expert opinion. This is a method of attacking the epistemic process used to reach the conclusion, not the conclusion's content directly.
explanation.A: A is incorrect. The editorial doesn't argue the two economists are less qualified.
explanation.B: B is incorrect. The editorial doesn't claim majority opinion should always prevail — it points out that the senator ignored the majority, which is a form of cherry-picking.
explanation.C: See correct explanation above.
explanation.D: D is incorrect. No alternative economic theory is offered.
explanation.E: E is incorrect. Whether tax cuts can stimulate growth at all is not questioned.

questions.stimulus: Historian: My colleague argues that the fall of the Roman Empire was primarily caused by military overextension. But this theory cannot explain why the Eastern Roman Empire — which faced identical military pressures — survived for nearly another thousand years. A satisfactory theory of Rome's fall must account for why the West collapsed when the East did not.
questions.question_stem: The historian's argument proceeds by

choices.A: offering an alternative explanation for why the Western Roman Empire fell
choices.B: identifying an asymmetric case that the colleague's theory fails to explain
choices.C: arguing that military overextension was not a significant factor in Rome's decline
choices.D: showing that the Eastern Empire's survival disproves the hypothesis of military overextension
choices.E: demonstrating that the colleague's theory is internally contradictory

explanation.correct: B is correct. The historian doesn't offer an alternative theory — just identifies a case (the Eastern Empire) that the colleague's theory cannot explain. If military overextension caused the fall, why did the East survive despite similar pressures? The asymmetry between East and West challenges the explanatory power of the theory.
explanation.A: A is incorrect. No alternative theory is provided.
explanation.B: See correct explanation above.
explanation.C: C is incorrect. The historian doesn't deny that military overextension was a factor — just that it's a primary/sufficient cause.
explanation.D: D is too strong. The Eastern survival shows the theory is incomplete, not that the factor is irrelevant.
explanation.E: E is incorrect. No internal contradiction is identified in the colleague's theory.

questions.stimulus: Anyone who has visited the new waterfront development agrees it is a success. Marcus has not visited the waterfront development. Therefore, Marcus has no basis for claiming it is not a success.
questions.question_stem: The argument proceeds by

choices.A: establishing a general principle and then applying it to a specific individual
choices.B: using an expert's endorsement to validate a policy outcome
choices.C: questioning the credibility of a critic based on a gap in their direct experience
choices.D: providing empirical evidence that the waterfront development has succeeded
choices.E: appealing to popular consensus as evidence of a factual claim

explanation.correct: C is correct. The argument doesn't address whether the development is actually a success — instead it argues that Marcus lacks standing to criticize because he hasn't visited. This is a method that challenges the critic's credentials (experiential knowledge) rather than engaging the substance of the criticism.
explanation.A: A is partially right (a principle is applied to Marcus) but mischaracterizes what the principle does — it's about disqualifying a critic, not establishing a factual conclusion.
explanation.B: B is incorrect. No expert is cited.
explanation.C: See correct explanation above.
explanation.D: D is incorrect. No evidence about actual success is provided.
explanation.E: E describes the first sentence's appeal to visitors but not the argument's overall method.

questions.stimulus: The city claims that its new noise ordinance is modeled on the successful ordinance enacted in Westfield. But Westfield's ordinance was effective primarily because it was enforced by a dedicated noise compliance unit funded by a federal grant. Our city has no such unit and no prospect of similar funding. The comparison to Westfield therefore provides little reason to expect our ordinance will be effective.
questions.question_stem: The argument counters the city's claim by

choices.A: arguing that noise pollution is not a significant problem in the city
choices.B: demonstrating that the city's ordinance differs substantively from Westfield's in key respects
choices.C: pointing out that conditions in Westfield that made its ordinance effective are absent here
choices.D: challenging the factual accuracy of the city's account of Westfield's experience
choices.E: showing that the city's ordinance was based on incomplete research into comparable policies

explanation.correct: C is correct. The argument accepts the Westfield comparison but undermines it by identifying a condition — dedicated enforcement funding — that was critical to Westfield's success but is absent locally. The analogy is weakened not by disputing the facts but by showing the key enabling condition doesn't transfer.
explanation.A: A is incorrect. The significance of noise pollution is not addressed.
explanation.B: B is close but not precise — the ordinances may be textually similar; the difference is in enforcement conditions, not the ordinance itself.
explanation.C: See correct explanation above.
explanation.D: D is incorrect. The argument doesn't dispute the Westfield facts.
explanation.E: E is incorrect. The quality of research isn't mentioned.

questions.stimulus: Nutritionist: My opponent argues that all saturated fat is harmful and should be avoided. But coconut oil, which is high in saturated fat, has been shown in several studies to raise HDL cholesterol — the so-called 'good' cholesterol — rather than the harmful LDL variety. This shows that blanket recommendations against all saturated fat are not scientifically justified.
questions.question_stem: The nutritionist's argument proceeds by

choices.A: demonstrating through controlled experiments that saturated fat is harmless
choices.B: using a specific case as a counterexample to challenge a general claim
choices.C: appealing to expert consensus to dispute a popular misconception
choices.D: arguing that HDL cholesterol is more important than LDL cholesterol
choices.E: redefining what counts as a harmful dietary substance

explanation.correct: B is correct. The nutritionist uses coconut oil as a specific counterexample to a general claim ('all saturated fat is harmful'). A single counterexample is sufficient to defeat a universal claim, which is the structure of this argument.
explanation.A: A is incorrect. The nutritionist cites studies but doesn't describe conducting controlled experiments, and the argument doesn't demonstrate that all saturated fat is harmless.
explanation.B: See correct explanation above.
explanation.C: C is incorrect. The nutritionist cites studies, not expert consensus, and is actually challenging a claim, not a misconception.
explanation.D: D is incorrect. The relative importance of HDL vs. LDL is not argued — only that this saturated fat raises HDL.
explanation.E: E is incorrect. No redefinition occurs.

questions.stimulus: All certified public accountants in the firm have passed the CPA exam. No one who has failed the CPA exam more than twice is permitted to retake it. Valentina has not passed the CPA exam and has failed it twice.
questions.question_stem: If the statements above are true, which of the following must also be true?

choices.A: Valentina will never become a certified public accountant
choices.B: Valentina is not currently a certified public accountant at the firm
choices.C: Valentina has failed the CPA exam the maximum allowable number of times
choices.D: Valentina will be permitted to retake the CPA exam one more time
choices.E: Some employees of the firm have not passed the CPA exam

explanation.correct: B must be true. The first sentence tells us all CPAs in the firm have passed the exam. Since Valentina has not passed, she cannot be a CPA at the firm.
explanation.A: A doesn't necessarily follow. Valentina has failed twice — the rule says those who have failed MORE THAN twice cannot retake it. She could still retake it a third time.
explanation.B: See correct explanation above.
explanation.C: C is incorrect. She has failed twice; the prohibition kicks in after failing more than twice. She's at, not beyond, a threshold — but the question is about the maximum, and the maximum isn't defined as twice in the passage.
explanation.D: D may be true but isn't proven — we only know she CAN retake it (hasn't failed more than twice), not that she WILL be permitted to by the firm.
explanation.E: E is plausible but not established. We know Valentina isn't a CPA at the firm, but we don't know if she's employed by the firm at all.

questions.stimulus: In a given ecosystem, predators regulate prey populations. When predators are removed, prey populations increase. When prey populations increase beyond the ecosystem's carrying capacity, vegetation is depleted. When vegetation is depleted, prey populations eventually collapse. The wolves were removed from the valley ten years ago.
questions.question_stem: Which of the following must be true based on the statements above?

choices.A: The prey population in the valley has already collapsed
choices.B: The vegetation in the valley has been depleted since the wolves were removed
choices.C: The prey population in the valley increased after the wolves were removed
choices.D: Without the return of wolves, the valley's ecosystem will never recover
choices.E: The valley's prey population exceeded the ecosystem's carrying capacity

explanation.correct: C must be true. The chain of conditionals says: predators removed → prey increases. The wolves (predators) were removed. Therefore, the prey population increased. This is a direct application of the first conditional.
explanation.A: A doesn't necessarily follow yet. The chain requires: prey increases → vegetation depleted → prey collapses. But we don't know whether the carrying capacity has been exceeded or vegetation depleted.
explanation.B: B requires an additional step — prey must have exceeded carrying capacity first, which isn't confirmed.
explanation.C: See correct explanation above.
explanation.D: D goes beyond the given conditionals — no information about recovery conditions is provided.
explanation.E: E may have occurred but isn't guaranteed by the given statements alone.

questions.stimulus: Every novel shortlisted for the Crestwood Prize this year was written by an author over 40. This year's Crestwood Prize winner was a first-time novelist. No first-time novelist over 40 has ever won the Crestwood Prize before this year.
questions.question_stem: If the above statements are true, which of the following must also be true?

choices.A: This year's Crestwood Prize winner is the youngest author ever to win the prize
choices.B: The Crestwood Prize has never before been won by a novel shortlisted this year
choices.C: This year's Crestwood Prize represents a first: a first-time novelist over 40 winning the prize
choices.D: This year's winner had previously published short stories or other non-novel works
choices.E: Future Crestwood Prize winners will also be first-time novelists over 40

explanation.correct: C must be true. Every shortlisted novel was by an author over 40 (so the winner is over 40). The winner was a first-time novelist. No first-time novelist over 40 had ever won before. Combining these: this year marks the first time a first-time novelist over 40 has won.
explanation.A: A is incorrect. 'Over 40' doesn't tell us how old the winner is — just that they're older than 40.
explanation.B: B is nonsensical — it misreads 'shortlisted this year' in a circular way.
explanation.C: See correct explanation above.
explanation.D: D is incorrect. 'First-time novelist' refers to first novel, not first publication — but even if it didn't, this isn't established.
explanation.E: E is incorrect. No prediction about future winners is supported.

questions.stimulus: Some historians specialize in ancient Rome. All historians who specialize in ancient Rome can read Latin. Dr. Petrov is a historian who cannot read Latin.
questions.question_stem: Which of the following can be properly inferred from the statements above?

choices.A: Dr. Petrov is not a well-regarded historian
choices.B: Dr. Petrov does not specialize in ancient Rome
choices.C: Dr. Petrov specializes in a period or region that does not require Latin
choices.D: All historians who cannot read Latin lack an important scholarly skill
