choices.E: No historian can specialize in ancient Rome without reading Latin

explanation.correct: B must be true. The contrapositive of 'all Rome specialists can read Latin' is 'if you cannot read Latin, you are not a Rome specialist.' Dr. Petrov cannot read Latin, so she is not a Rome specialist.
explanation.A: A is incorrect and unsupported — the argument says nothing about reputation.
explanation.B: See correct explanation above.
explanation.C: C may be true but isn't proven — we don't know what Dr. Petrov specializes in.
explanation.D: D goes beyond the argument's scope — it makes a value judgment not supported by the premises.
explanation.E: E restates one of the premises and is true, but is not something inferred from the statements — it is one of the statements.

questions.stimulus: A software company offers three products: a word processor, a spreadsheet application, and a database manager. Any customer who purchases the database manager also purchases the spreadsheet application. No customer who has purchased the word processor has also purchased the database manager. Tomás has purchased exactly two of the three products.
questions.question_stem: Which of the following must be true?

choices.A: Tomás has purchased the spreadsheet application
choices.B: Tomás has not purchased the database manager
choices.C: Tomás has purchased the word processor and the spreadsheet application
choices.D: Tomás has purchased the word processor
choices.E: Tomás has purchased the database manager and the spreadsheet application

explanation.correct: A must be true. Consider the two possible pairs Tomás could own: (1) word processor + spreadsheet, (2) word processor + database manager, or (3) spreadsheet + database manager. Pair (2) is ruled out because no word processor buyer also buys the database manager. That leaves pair (1) or (3). In pair (1), Tomás has the spreadsheet. In pair (3), Tomás also has the spreadsheet. Either way, Tomás has the spreadsheet.
explanation.A: See correct explanation above.
explanation.B: B is not necessarily true — pair (3) includes the database manager.
explanation.C: C is not necessarily true — pair (3) is also possible.
explanation.D: D is not necessarily true — pair (3) does not include the word processor.
explanation.E: E is not necessarily true — pair (1) is also possible.

questions.stimulus: In a survey of 200 employees, every employee who reported high job satisfaction also reported feeling respected by their manager. Half the employees reported high job satisfaction. Of those who did not report high job satisfaction, 60 percent reported feeling respected by their manager.
questions.question_stem: Based on the above information, which of the following must be true?

choices.A: More than half of the 200 employees feel respected by their manager
choices.B: Feeling respected by one's manager causes high job satisfaction
choices.C: Employees who feel respected by their manager always have high job satisfaction
choices.D: Half of all employees surveyed feel respected by their manager
choices.E: The majority of employees with high job satisfaction have good relationships with their managers

explanation.correct: A must be true. 100 employees have high satisfaction and all feel respected (100). Of the other 100, 60% feel respected (60). Total who feel respected: 100 + 60 = 160 out of 200 — more than half.
explanation.A: See correct explanation above.
explanation.B: B is a causal claim not supported by survey data, which only shows correlation.
explanation.C: C is the reverse of what's stated — all satisfied employees feel respected, but many non-satisfied employees also feel respected.
explanation.D: D is arithmetic error — the actual number is 160, not 100.
explanation.E: E restates part of the premise but vaguely — all high-satisfaction employees feel respected, which is stronger than 'majority.'

questions.stimulus: No species can survive in an environment where the average temperature falls below its thermal tolerance minimum for more than three consecutive months. The thermal tolerance minimum for the Ridley's gecko is 15°C. Last winter, the average temperature in the Northern Plateau region fell below 15°C for five consecutive months.
questions.question_stem: Which of the following must be true on the basis of the statements above?

choices.A: The Ridley's gecko population in the Northern Plateau is extinct
choices.B: Ridley's geckos cannot survive anywhere with cold winters
choices.C: Ridley's geckos did not survive in the Northern Plateau region last winter
choices.D: No reptiles can survive in the Northern Plateau region
choices.E: The Northern Plateau's climate is unsuitable for most gecko species

explanation.correct: C must be true. The conditions stated: below thermal minimum for more than 3 months → cannot survive. Northern Plateau was below 15°C for 5 months (more than 3). Ridley's gecko minimum is 15°C. Therefore: Ridley's geckos could not survive there last winter.
explanation.A: A goes too far — the geckos may not have been present in the Northern Plateau at all, or may have migrated. They didn't survive there last winter, but extinction is not established.
explanation.B: B generalizes too broadly — the argument is about one region in one winter.
explanation.C: See correct explanation above.
explanation.D: D is incorrect — no claim is made about other reptile species.
explanation.E: E is not supported — no comparison to other gecko species is made.

questions.stimulus: The only factors that ever lead to a company's stock price increasing significantly within a single trading day are either an unexpectedly strong earnings report or a major acquisition announcement. Today, NovaTech's stock increased significantly. NovaTech's earnings report released this morning came in exactly at analyst expectations.
questions.question_stem: If the statements above are true, which of the following must also be true?

choices.A: NovaTech's stock increase will persist over the following week
choices.B: NovaTech made a major acquisition announcement today
choices.C: NovaTech's strong earnings are more significant than the market expected
choices.D: The analyst expectations for NovaTech were too conservative
choices.E: NovaTech's stock will continue to increase in the next trading session

explanation.correct: B must be true. The argument gives an exhaustive disjunction: significant single-day stock increases are caused ONLY by (A) unexpectedly strong earnings or (B) major acquisition. NovaTech's stock rose significantly. Its earnings were NOT unexpectedly strong (came in at expectations). Therefore, the other disjunct must be true: a major acquisition announcement occurred.
explanation.A: A is not supported — single-day and multi-day performance are not linked.
explanation.B: See correct explanation above.
explanation.C: C contradicts the premise — earnings were at expectations, not stronger.
explanation.D: D is incorrect — meeting expectations means expectations were accurate.
explanation.E: E is not supported — no information about future sessions is given.

questions.stimulus: Fluoride is added to municipal water supplies to prevent tooth decay. Studies show that communities with fluoridated water have significantly lower rates of tooth decay than those without. Critics argue that fluoride at certain doses has adverse health effects. Proponents respond that the doses used in water fluoridation are well below levels associated with any adverse effects.
questions.question_stem: Which of the following is most strongly supported by the statements above?

choices.A: Water fluoridation is safe and should be implemented in all municipalities
choices.B: The doses of fluoride used in water fluoridation are considered safe by those who support the practice
choices.C: Critics of water fluoridation are incorrect about adverse health effects at any dose
choices.D: Communities without fluoridated water have worse overall public health outcomes
choices.E: The primary purpose of adding fluoride to water is to benefit children's dental health

explanation.correct: B is the most strongly supported. The proponents specifically say fluoridation doses are below levels with adverse effects. B accurately captures this claim as coming from proponents — it doesn't assert that this is objectively true, only that this is what proponents argue.
explanation.A: A makes a normative claim ('should be implemented') that goes beyond the evidence given.
explanation.B: See correct explanation above.
explanation.C: C misrepresents the proponents' argument — they say THESE doses are safe, not that fluoride is safe at any dose.
explanation.D: D overstates — lower tooth decay rates don't imply worse overall public health.
explanation.E: E introduces a claim about children specifically that isn't stated in the argument.

questions.stimulus: The more frequently a foreign language is used in daily life, the more proficient a speaker becomes. Language immersion programs, by maximizing daily use, therefore produce higher proficiency than classroom-only instruction. However, immersion programs require either living in a country where the language is spoken or access to a specialized immersion school, neither of which is available to most language learners.
questions.question_stem: Which of the following conclusions is most strongly supported by the passage?

choices.A: Classroom instruction is ineffective for achieving language proficiency
choices.B: For most language learners, the optimal method for achieving proficiency is unavailable
choices.C: Immersion programs are the only reliable path to language proficiency
choices.D: Language proficiency is primarily determined by access to native-speaking communities
choices.E: Most language learners will not achieve the proficiency levels possible through immersion programs

explanation.correct: E is most strongly supported. The argument establishes that immersion produces the highest proficiency and that most learners can't access immersion programs. Combining these: most learners will not achieve immersion-level proficiency.
explanation.A: A is too strong — the argument says immersion is better, not that classroom instruction is ineffective.
explanation.B: B is close but slightly imprecise — 'optimal method' being unavailable is true but E more directly captures the proficiency consequence.
explanation.C: C is too strong — the argument doesn't say immersion is the ONLY reliable path.
explanation.D: D is not stated — the argument talks about daily use, not specifically native-speaking communities.
explanation.E: See correct explanation above.

questions.stimulus: The Westbrook Theater has seen declining ticket sales over the past three years. The management has decided to invest in a complete renovation of the lobby and seating areas. They argue that the renovation will reverse the decline in ticket sales.
questions.question_stem: The management's argument depends on which of the following assumptions?

choices.A: The renovation will be completed within the next fiscal year
choices.B: The theater's declining ticket sales are at least partly due to the condition of the physical facility
choices.C: Other theaters in the area have not recently undergone similar renovations
choices.D: The renovation will not disrupt performances during the construction period
choices.E: Patrons who previously attended the theater were satisfied with the artistic programming

explanation.correct: B is the necessary assumption. The argument assumes that renovating the facility will reverse ticket declines — but this only works if poor facility conditions contributed to the decline in the first place. If the decline is due to programming, location, pricing, or other factors unrelated to the physical space, the renovation would not address the actual cause. Negate B: if the physical condition has nothing to do with the decline, the renovation cannot logically reverse it.
explanation.A: A is not necessary. The argument doesn't depend on a specific timeline.
explanation.B: See correct explanation above.
explanation.C: C is not necessary. Competitor behavior is not assumed in the argument.
explanation.D: D is a practical concern but not logically necessary for the conclusion.
explanation.E: E is not assumed — whether past patrons liked the programming is irrelevant to whether facility improvements would bring people back.

questions.stimulus: The mayor argues that expanding the city's bike lane network will reduce automobile traffic and thus lower carbon emissions. She points to a study showing that in cities with extensive bike lane networks, car use per capita is lower than in cities without such networks.
questions.question_stem: The mayor's argument requires assuming which of the following?

choices.A: Residents who currently drive will switch to cycling once more bike lanes are available
choices.B: Building bike lanes does not require removing existing car lanes
choices.C: The study cited compared cities with otherwise similar demographics and infrastructure
choices.D: Carbon emissions from automobiles are the primary contributor to the city's pollution
choices.E: Cycling is a practical mode of transportation for most of the city's residents for their regular commutes

explanation.correct: E is the necessary assumption. The mayor's argument requires that cycling is a viable alternative for residents — otherwise, adding bike lanes would not reduce car use. If the city's geography, weather, distances, or demographics make cycling impractical for most residents, expanding bike infrastructure won't shift behavior. Negate E: if cycling isn't practical for most residents, adding lanes won't change their transportation choices, and the conclusion fails.
explanation.A: A is close but too specific. The argument requires that SOME shift is possible — E captures this more broadly.
explanation.B: B is a practical concern but not logically necessary for the conclusion.
explanation.C: C would strengthen the evidential quality of the study but isn't logically required by the mayor's argument.
explanation.D: D introduces automobiles as the primary pollution source — the argument only claims reducing car use reduces emissions, not that cars are the primary source.
explanation.E: See correct explanation above.

questions.stimulus: Philosopher: Free will is impossible if all events are determined by prior causes. Neuroscience has shown that every decision a human being makes is preceded by measurable brain activity that predicts the decision before the person is consciously aware of it. Therefore, human beings do not have free will.
questions.question_stem: The philosopher's argument depends on which of the following assumptions?

choices.A: Neuroscientists can reliably predict all human decisions from brain activity alone
choices.B: Consciousness is required for a decision to be freely made
choices.C: Brain activity that precedes conscious awareness constitutes a prior cause that determines the decision
choices.D: Free will requires that decisions are not influenced by any prior mental states
choices.E: Neuroscience will eventually provide a complete account of human decision-making

explanation.correct: C is the necessary assumption. The argument moves from 'brain activity precedes and predicts the decision' to 'therefore decisions are determined by prior causes.' This step requires assuming that pre-conscious brain activity constitutes deterministic causation — not merely prediction or correlation. Without this assumption, the neuroscience finding is consistent with free will.
explanation.A: A overstates the neuroscience claim — the argument says activity 'predicts,' not that prediction is complete or universal.
explanation.B: B is a possible interpretation of free will but isn't the logical bridge needed in this argument.
explanation.C: See correct explanation above.
explanation.D: D is too strong and not what the argument assumes — free will as defined here just requires not being fully determined.
explanation.E: E is an aspiration, not a necessary assumption for the current argument.

questions.stimulus: Since our company's productivity increased by 20 percent following the introduction of flexible work hours, we should implement flexible work hours across all departments to increase overall company productivity.
questions.question_stem: The argument assumes which of the following?

choices.A: Flexible work hours are preferred by all employees over fixed schedules
choices.B: The departments that have not yet adopted flexible hours would benefit from the change similarly to the departments that have
choices.C: Productivity increased in all departments that adopted flexible work hours, not just some of them
choices.D: The company's competitors do not already offer flexible work hours
choices.E: The 20 percent productivity increase is attributable solely to flexible work hours and not to other concurrent changes

explanation.correct: B is the necessary assumption. The argument extrapolates from the initial implementation to all remaining departments. This only works if the policy would have similar effects on the uninitiated departments — they must be comparable to the ones where it already worked. Negate B: if the other departments would not benefit similarly, expanding the policy wouldn't achieve the stated goal.
explanation.A: A is not necessary. The argument is about productivity, not employee preference.
explanation.B: See correct explanation above.
explanation.C: C is a plausible concern but not logically necessary — the argument could work even if not all departments benefited equally.
explanation.D: D is irrelevant to whether expanding the policy would increase productivity.
explanation.E: E is a legitimate concern about causal attribution but goes beyond the logical structure of this specific argument.

questions.stimulus: Critics claim that the new high-speed rail line will reduce regional air travel. However, for the high-speed rail to compete with air travel, it must serve the same major cities as the regional airlines. The proposed rail line stops only in midsize cities that the airlines do not serve. Therefore, the rail line will not reduce regional air travel.
questions.question_stem: The argument relies on which of the following assumptions?

choices.A: High-speed rail and air travel cannot be complementary modes of transportation
choices.B: Travelers going to the midsize cities served by the rail line do not currently fly to nearby major city airports and then travel onward
choices.C: The rail line will not be extended to major cities in the future
choices.D: Air travelers prefer speed over cost when choosing transportation
choices.E: Airlines are the only competitors that high-speed rail should be concerned with

explanation.correct: B is the necessary assumption. The argument says rail doesn't serve the same cities as airlines. But if travelers to midsize cities currently fly to a nearby major airport and then drive or take a bus, the new rail line could capture those travelers — reducing regional air travel. The argument assumes these travelers don't currently use the air-then-transfer pattern. Negate B: if they do, the rail line would reduce air travel, defeating the conclusion.
explanation.A: A is too broad — the argument doesn't require that rail and air can never complement each other.
explanation.B: See correct explanation above.
explanation.C: C is a temporal consideration not required for the current argument's logic.
explanation.D: D is an irrelevant claim about traveler preferences.
explanation.E: E is too strong and not assumed by the argument.

questions.stimulus: Research shows that students who take notes by hand retain material better than those who type notes on laptops. Our school should therefore ban laptops in classrooms to improve academic performance.
questions.question_stem: Which of the following is an assumption required by this argument?

choices.A: Handwritten notes are more legible than typed notes for most students
choices.B: Banning laptops will cause students to take notes by hand rather than not take notes at all
choices.C: The research on note-taking methods controlled for student ability and study habits
choices.D: Improved material retention is the most important factor in academic performance
choices.E: Laptops are used primarily for note-taking rather than other academic tasks

explanation.correct: B is the necessary assumption. The argument assumes that removing laptops leads to the beneficial behavior (handwriting). But students might respond to a laptop ban by not taking notes at all, or by using phones, or by disengaging. The argument requires that the ban actually produces the handwriting behavior. Negate B: if students don't switch to handwriting after a ban, the conclusion fails.
explanation.A: A is not assumed — the argument is about retention, not legibility.
explanation.B: See correct explanation above.
explanation.C: C is a methodological concern about the research quality but isn't strictly necessary for the argument's logic.
explanation.D: D is not assumed — the argument only claims the policy will improve performance, not that retention is the only factor.
explanation.E: E is interesting but not necessary — even if laptops have other uses, the argument's logic only requires that removing them shifts note-taking behavior.

questions.stimulus: The only way to ensure a fair trial is to have jurors who are completely impartial. News coverage of a criminal case before trial inevitably biases potential jurors. Therefore, extensive pre-trial news coverage makes a fair trial impossible.
questions.question_stem: The argument depends on assuming which of the following?

choices.A: Jurors who have been exposed to news coverage cannot be identified and excluded during jury selection
choices.B: Judicial instructions are insufficient to overcome pre-trial bias in jurors
choices.C: Pre-trial news coverage always creates bias that cannot be remedied through any procedural means
choices.D: A fair trial requires more than just impartial jurors
choices.E: Defendants are always disadvantaged by pre-trial news coverage

explanation.correct: C is the necessary assumption. The argument concludes that extensive coverage makes fair trial 'impossible' — a strong claim. The argument needs to assume that the bias created by coverage cannot be remedied (through voir dire, change of venue, jury instructions, etc.). If bias could be screened out or corrected, fair trials would still be possible despite coverage. Negate C: if bias can be remedied procedurally, the conclusion that fair trial is impossible fails.
explanation.A: A describes one remedy (exclusion) but C is more comprehensive and necessary.
explanation.B: B is a specific procedural remedy being ruled out, but C more broadly covers all remedies.
explanation.C: See correct explanation above.
explanation.D: D contradicts the argument's first premise.
explanation.E: E is not assumed — the argument doesn't specify direction of bias.

questions.stimulus: Companies that have a diverse workforce are more innovative than those that do not. Our company wants to become more innovative. Therefore, our company should prioritize workforce diversity.
questions.question_stem: The argument assumes which of the following?

choices.A: Diversity causes innovation rather than merely being correlated with it
choices.B: Our company's current workforce is not already diverse
choices.C: Innovation cannot be achieved through means other than workforce diversity
choices.D: A more innovative company will necessarily be more profitable
choices.E: The type of diversity found in other companies is achievable at our company

explanation.correct: A is the necessary assumption. The argument moves from the correlation between diversity and innovation to the prescription that we should pursue diversity to achieve innovation. This only works if diversity actually causes the innovation — otherwise, pursuing diversity wouldn't produce the desired result. Negate A: if diversity and innovation merely correlate (with some third factor causing both), pursuing diversity wouldn't increase innovation.
explanation.A: See correct explanation above.
explanation.B: B is not strictly necessary — even if already diverse, perhaps more diversity produces more innovation.
explanation.C: C is too strong. The argument recommends diversity as a means but doesn't assume it's the only means.
explanation.D: D introduces profitability not mentioned in the argument.
explanation.E: E is a practical concern but not strictly required for the logical structure.

questions.stimulus: A study found that children who participate in organized sports have better social skills than those who do not. Parents should therefore enroll their children in organized sports to improve their social skills.
questions.question_stem: The recommendation above assumes which of the following?

choices.A: Social skills are more important than academic achievement for children's future success
choices.B: Children who are enrolled in organized sports will actually participate actively
choices.C: The social skills benefits of organized sports are not outweighed by the risks of injury
choices.D: Children with better social skills are not simply more likely to choose to participate in organized sports
choices.E: All organized sports provide equal opportunities for developing social skills

explanation.correct: D is the necessary assumption. The study shows that sports participants have better social skills. But the recommendation assumes this is because sports builds social skills — not because socially skilled children are more likely to join sports. If socially skilled kids self-select into sports, enrolling less socially skilled children wouldn't improve their skills. Negate D: if better social skills cause sports participation (not vice versa), the recommendation fails.
explanation.A: A is not assumed — the argument doesn't need to prioritize social skills over academics.
explanation.B: B is a practical concern but not the core logical assumption.
explanation.C: C is a relevant consideration but not required for the argument's logical structure.
explanation.D: See correct explanation above.
explanation.E: E is too strong — the argument doesn't require equal benefits across all sports.

questions.stimulus: The historical evidence shows that democracies rarely go to war against other democracies. Researchers have therefore concluded that spreading democracy globally would significantly reduce the frequency of international wars.
questions.question_stem: The researchers' conclusion requires which of the following assumptions?

choices.A: Democracies are inherently more peaceful than authoritarian regimes in all conflicts
choices.B: The historical trend of democratic peace would persist even as democracy spreads to new regions and cultures
choices.C: International organizations have no significant effect on preventing wars between states
choices.D: Countries that transition to democracy do not face increased risk of civil war during the transition period
choices.E: Economic interdependence between democracies is the primary mechanism explaining the democratic peace

explanation.correct: B is the necessary assumption. The argument extrapolates from a historical pattern to a future policy prediction. This requires assuming that the pattern would generalize to new contexts — new democracies in different regions, cultures, and geopolitical environments. Negate B: if the democratic peace pattern doesn't hold as democracy spreads to new regions, the conclusion that global democratization would reduce war fails.
explanation.A: A is too strong and not required — the claim is specifically about wars between democracies, not all conflicts.
explanation.B: See correct explanation above.
explanation.C: C is irrelevant to whether democracy itself reduces war.
explanation.D: D is a legitimate concern about transition risks but doesn't undermine the core conclusion about mature democracies.
explanation.E: E addresses the mechanism, which isn't required for the argument's logical conclusion.

questions.stimulus: Since every employee who was promoted last year had been with the company for at least three years, we can conclude that Simone, who has been with the company for only one year, was not promoted last year.
questions.question_stem: Which of the following arguments has a parallel logical structure to the argument above?

choices.A: All winning entries in the competition were submitted before the deadline. Therefore, any submission made before the deadline is a winning entry.
choices.B: Every marathon finisher trained for at least six months. Rodrigo trained for only four months. Therefore, Rodrigo did not finish the marathon.
choices.C: Most applicants to the program have graduate degrees. Anya does not have a graduate degree. Therefore, Anya was probably not accepted.
choices.D: All board members attended the annual meeting. Since Callum attended the annual meeting, Callum must be a board member.
choices.E: No one who failed the entrance exam was admitted. Priya did not fail the entrance exam. Therefore, Priya was admitted.

explanation.correct: B is correct. The original argument: All promoted employees had 3+ years experience (necessary condition). Simone has <3 years. Therefore, Simone was not promoted. This is modus tollens: All X→Y; not-Y; therefore not-X. B: All marathon finishers trained 6+ months. Rodrigo trained <6 months. Therefore, Rodrigo didn't finish. Same structure exactly.
explanation.A: A reverses the logic — it treats the condition as sufficient when it was necessary. This is an error, not a parallel to the (valid) original.
explanation.B: See correct explanation above.
explanation.C: C uses 'most' rather than 'all' and 'probably' — a probabilistic, not deductive, structure.
explanation.D: D also reverses the logic (affirming the consequent).
explanation.E: E has an additional step — it negates the wrong half of the conditional.

questions.stimulus: The city has not repaired the bridge in over a decade. If the city hasn't repaired a structure in over a decade, it must no longer consider that structure a priority. Therefore, the city no longer considers the bridge a priority.
questions.question_stem: Which of the following most closely parallels the reasoning in the argument above?

choices.A: The university has not updated its curriculum in five years. Universities that fail to update curricula lose accreditation. Therefore, this university will lose accreditation.
choices.B: The foundation has not responded to the grant application in three months. Foundations that haven't responded in three months typically deny the application. Therefore, the application was probably denied.
choices.C: The department hasn't hired anyone new in two years. When organizations don't hire for two years, it means they are in a hiring freeze. Therefore, the department is in a hiring freeze.
choices.D: The hospital has not replaced its MRI machine since 2015. All hospitals need functioning MRI machines. Therefore, this hospital's MRI machine may be outdated.
choices.E: The team has not won a game in six weeks. Teams that don't win for six weeks are typically eliminated from playoff contention. Therefore, the team is unlikely to make the playoffs.

explanation.correct: C has the same structure: [Subject] has not done X in Y time → If you haven't done X in Y time, it means Z → Therefore, [Subject] is Z. The argument uses a general principle connecting the absence of behavior to a conclusion about current status. C follows this exactly.
explanation.A: A introduces a different consequence (loss of accreditation) that isn't derived from the inaction itself but from a separate rule.
explanation.B: B uses 'probably' and 'typically,' making it probabilistic rather than deductive.
explanation.C: See correct explanation above.
explanation.D: D doesn't use a general principle connecting inaction to a status conclusion — it just infers possible outdatedness.
explanation.E: E is probabilistic ('unlikely') and based on performance trend, not a definitional connection like the original.

questions.stimulus: We should either invest in renewable energy infrastructure or face continued dependence on fossil fuels. Since abandoning fossil fuel dependence is essential for meeting our climate commitments, we must invest in renewable energy infrastructure.
questions.question_stem: Which of the following most closely parallels the logical structure of the argument above?

choices.A: We should either reduce spending or raise taxes. Since raising taxes is politically infeasible, we should reduce spending.
choices.B: Either the defendant committed the crime or someone else did. Since someone else could not have committed it alone, the defendant must have been involved.
choices.C: Either the product is defective or the user is at fault. Since user fault would not explain the pattern of failures, the product must be defective.
choices.D: We must choose between speed and accuracy in this report. Since accuracy is required for our client's decision-making, we must prioritize accuracy over speed.
choices.E: Either we update the software or our systems will become vulnerable. Since vulnerability would expose us to liability, we must update the software.

explanation.correct: E matches the original structure: Either A or B. Not-B is required (meeting climate commitments/avoiding liability). Therefore A. The original argues: Either invest in renewables OR remain fossil-fuel dependent. Remaining fossil-fuel dependent is incompatible with climate commitments. Therefore invest in renewables. E: Either update software OR systems become vulnerable. Vulnerability is unacceptable. Therefore update software.
explanation.A: A uses the disjunction by ruling out one option as infeasible — different reasoning (feasibility, not goal-incompatibility).
explanation.B: B introduces a more complex structure about partial involvement.
explanation.C: C rules out one option based on its explanatory inadequacy, not incompatibility with a goal.
explanation.D: D frames the choice as a priority tradeoff rather than ruling out one disjunct based on goal incompatibility.
explanation.E: See correct explanation above.

questions.stimulus: This restaurant has received a five-star health inspection rating every year for the past decade. Any restaurant that consistently receives five-star ratings must maintain rigorous sanitation standards. Therefore, this restaurant maintains rigorous sanitation standards.
questions.question_stem: Which of the following has a parallel logical structure?

choices.A: This athlete has won every competition she has entered for the last five years. Athletes who win consistently are among the world's best. Therefore, this athlete is among the world's best.
choices.B: This bridge has held up under maximum load conditions for 50 years. Any bridge that can hold maximum load for 50 years was designed by a competent engineer. Therefore, a competent engineer designed this bridge.
choices.C: This software has never crashed during beta testing. Software that never crashes is ready for full release. Therefore, this software is ready for full release.
choices.D: All of the above have parallel logical structures.
choices.E: None of the above have parallel logical structures.

explanation.correct: D is correct. All three options (A, B, C) share the same logical structure as the original: [Subject] has consistently demonstrated property X over time. Any entity that consistently demonstrates X has property Y. Therefore, [Subject] has property Y. This is a valid categorical syllogism pattern applied across three different domains.
explanation.A: Matches the structure.
explanation.B: Matches the structure.
explanation.C: Matches the structure.
explanation.D: See correct explanation above.
explanation.E: E is incorrect — all three match.

questions.stimulus: Some advocates for criminal justice reform argue for eliminating cash bail. But bail reform opponents note that some defendants released without bail reoffend before trial. One cannot infer, however, that because some bail-free defendants reoffend, eliminating cash bail will cause more crime overall, since only a small percentage of defendants actually reoffend, and cash bail disproportionately detains low-income defendants who pose minimal risk.
questions.question_stem: The pattern of reasoning in the above argument is most similar to which of the following?

choices.A: Some people who take a new medication experience side effects. One cannot infer that the medication causes widespread harm, since the side effects are minor and affect only a small percentage of patients who benefit greatly from the drug.
choices.B: Some immigrants commit crimes. One cannot conclude that immigration increases crime, since immigrants commit crimes at lower rates than native-born citizens and contribute substantially to economic growth.
choices.C: Some students who attend large universities feel isolated. One cannot conclude that large universities are inferior, since most students thrive socially and academically at these institutions.
choices.D: Some electric vehicles have caught fire. One cannot infer that electric vehicles are more dangerous than gasoline vehicles, since gasoline vehicle fires occur far more frequently per vehicle mile traveled.
choices.E: Some organic foods have been found to contain pesticides. One cannot conclude that organic labeling is meaningless, since the levels detected are typically below regulatory thresholds.

explanation.correct: A most closely parallels the original's structure: (1) Acknowledge a specific real negative outcome affecting some (defendants reoffend / patients have side effects), (2) Reject the inference that this proves the policy/drug causes overall harm, (3) Cite that the affected group is small and the overall balance of effects is positive. A follows this pattern precisely.
explanation.A: See correct explanation above.
explanation.B: B adds a comparison to a baseline (native-born crime rates) that the original doesn't use.
explanation.C: C doesn't include the crucial third element of showing positive overall benefit.
explanation.D: D uses a comparative safety statistic as the rebuttal — the original's structure is different.
explanation.E: E addresses a different type of concern (label accuracy vs. harm causation).

questions.stimulus: No competent engineer would approve a design that violates safety codes. This bridge design violates three safety codes. Therefore, the engineer who approved this design is not competent.
questions.question_stem: Which of the following most closely parallels the reasoning above?

choices.A: A responsible doctor would not prescribe a medication known to be dangerous. This doctor prescribed such a medication. Therefore, this doctor is not responsible.
choices.B: All engineers who violate safety codes face disciplinary action. This engineer violated safety codes. Therefore, this engineer will face disciplinary action.
choices.C: Most well-trained surgeons avoid unnecessary procedures. This surgeon performed an unnecessary procedure. Therefore, this surgeon may not be well-trained.
choices.D: No ethical journalist fabricates quotes. This journalist has been accused of fabricating quotes. Therefore, this journalist behaved unethically.
choices.E: A qualified accountant would not make arithmetic errors. This accountant made an arithmetic error. Therefore, this accountant is not qualified.

explanation.correct: E matches the exact structure: No [qualified member of profession] would do X. This [member] did X. Therefore, this [member] is not [qualified]. The original: No competent engineer would approve X. This engineer approved X. Therefore, not competent.
explanation.A: A uses 'responsible' rather than 'competent' and 'prescribed' rather than 'approved,' but matches closely. However, E is an even closer match.
explanation.B: B is a different structure (all→will face consequences), not a parallel to the original.
explanation.C: C uses 'most' and 'may' — probabilistic, not deductive.
explanation.D: D works but 'accused' introduces uncertainty — the person may not have actually fabricated quotes.
explanation.E: See correct explanation above.

questions.stimulus: The government will either raise the minimum wage or workers at the bottom of the income distribution will fall further behind inflation. The government has announced it will not raise the minimum wage this year. Consequently, workers at the bottom of the income distribution will fall further behind inflation.
questions.question_stem: Which of the following most closely parallels the logical structure of the argument?

choices.A: The board will approve the merger or the company will face bankruptcy. The board has approved the merger. Therefore, the company will not face bankruptcy.
choices.B: Either the levee holds or the town will flood. The town has flooded. Therefore, the levee did not hold.
choices.C: The hospital will expand its ICU capacity or it will be unable to handle a surge in patients. The hospital has decided not to expand ICU capacity. Therefore, it will be unable to handle a surge in patients.
choices.D: Either the product launch succeeds or the company will need to restructure. The company has decided to restructure. Therefore, the product launch did not succeed.
choices.E: Either the witness is telling the truth or the defendant is innocent. The witness has been shown to be lying. Therefore, the defendant is innocent.

explanation.correct: C has the same structure: Either A or B. Not-A. Therefore B. Original: Either raise minimum wage (A) or workers fall behind (B). Not-A. Therefore B. C: Either expand ICU (A) or unable to handle surge (B). Not-A. Therefore B.
explanation.A: A affirms A and denies B — a different application of the disjunction (also an error — the disjunction could be inclusive).
explanation.B: B starts with B being true and concludes not-A — this is the contrapositive application (also valid but different structure).
explanation.C: See correct explanation above.
explanation.D: D starts with B being true and concludes not-A — same as B's pattern.
explanation.E: E uses not-A to conclude B — same as C's structure, but the argument's logic is questionable. C is still more precisely parallel to the original.

questions.stimulus: The fact that a treatment works in a clinical trial does not guarantee it will work in the general population. Clinical trials use carefully selected participants who often do not represent the full diversity of patients who will eventually receive the treatment. Therefore, we should exercise caution before concluding that clinical trial results translate directly into real-world effectiveness.
questions.question_stem: Which of the following most closely parallels the argument's structure and reasoning strategy?

choices.A: The fact that an investment performed well over the last decade does not guarantee it will perform well in the next decade. Past markets operated under different conditions than today's. Therefore, investors should not assume past performance predicts future results.
choices.B: Just because a law works in one country does not mean it will work in another. Countries have different political and cultural contexts. Therefore, policymakers should carefully study whether foreign policies are transferable before adopting them.
choices.C: Simply because a teaching method works in elite private schools does not mean it will work in underfunded public schools. The student populations and available resources differ greatly. Therefore, education reformers should be cautious about applying private-school models to public schools.
choices.D: The fact that a new vaccine is effective against one strain of a virus does not guarantee it will work against mutated strains. Therefore, ongoing vaccine development is necessary.
choices.E: A product that performs well in laboratory testing does not necessarily perform as well in real-world conditions. Laboratory environments control for variables that exist in the real world. Therefore, we should conduct field testing before mass production.

explanation.correct: C most closely mirrors both the structure and the specific reasoning strategy: (1) Performance in a controlled/selective context (clinical trial / elite private school) doesn't guarantee the same in a broader context. (2) The reason: the controlled group isn't representative of the broader population (carefully selected patients / different student populations and resources). (3) Recommendation: exercise caution about generalizing. C matches all three elements.
explanation.A: A uses a temporal non-representativeness argument (past ≠ future conditions), not a population representativeness argument.
explanation.B: B is close but the non-representativeness is due to political/cultural context, not selection of participants.
explanation.C: See correct explanation above.
explanation.D: D lacks the representativeness mechanism — it's about biological variation, not sample selection.
explanation.E: E is structurally similar but lacks the specific representativeness mechanism about who is in the test group.

questions.stimulus: A law that is rarely enforced is effectively no law at all, since laws that are never applied have no deterrent effect. The city's anti-idling ordinance, which prohibits vehicles from idling for more than three minutes, is almost never enforced. Therefore, the anti-idling ordinance is effectively no law at all.
questions.question_stem: Which of the following most closely parallels the reasoning?

choices.A: A rule that no one follows is effectively meaningless. The company's email communication policy is widely ignored. Therefore, the policy is effectively meaningless.
choices.B: A manager who never gives feedback is effectively no manager at all. This manager has not given any employee feedback this year. Therefore, this manager is effectively performing no managerial function.
choices.C: A bridge that is never used serves no transportation function. The new pedestrian bridge is rarely used. Therefore, the pedestrian bridge serves little transportation function.
choices.D: A contract that is never honored is effectively void. This supplier has never fulfilled its contractual obligations. Therefore, this contract is effectively void.
choices.E: A skill that is never practiced atrophies. This employee has not used her data analysis skills in two years. Therefore, her data analysis skills have atrophied.

explanation.correct: A most closely matches. Original: General principle (law rarely enforced = no law). Application (anti-idling = rarely enforced). Conclusion (anti-idling = no law). A: General principle (rule no one follows = meaningless). Application (email policy = widely ignored). Conclusion (policy = meaningless). Same three-part structure.
explanation.A: See correct explanation above.
explanation.B: B is close but the middle term differs — 'no feedback' is the condition, not the rare enforcement parallel.
explanation.C: C uses 'rarely used' which better parallels 'rarely enforced' — but the general principle in C is about transportation function, not about deterrence/meaning.
explanation.D: D uses 'never honored' (stronger than 'rarely') and introduces the specific legal concept of 'void.'
explanation.E: E uses a different causal structure (non-use causes atrophy) rather than a functional equivalence argument.

questions.stimulus: A species that cannot adapt to changing environmental conditions will eventually go extinct. Genetic diversity within a species increases its ability to adapt. The cheetah has extremely low genetic diversity. Therefore, unless conditions remain stable, the cheetah is at risk of extinction.
questions.question_stem: Which of the following most closely parallels the logical structure above?

choices.A: An economy unable to diversify will contract during a recession. Export diversification improves economic resilience. This country exports only one commodity. Therefore, unless commodity prices remain stable, this country's economy is at risk of severe contraction.
choices.B: A building that cannot withstand earthquakes will collapse in a major seismic event. Reinforced foundations increase earthquake resistance. This building lacks reinforced foundations. Therefore, this building will collapse in an earthquake.
choices.C: An athlete who cannot recover from injuries will have a short career. Flexibility training reduces injury risk. This athlete does not do flexibility training. Therefore, this athlete is likely to have a short career.
choices.D: A company that cannot innovate will fail. Having a dedicated R&D budget promotes innovation. This company has no R&D budget. Therefore, this company will eventually fail.
choices.E: A bridge that corrodes will become structurally unsafe. Protective coatings prevent corrosion. This bridge lacks protective coatings. Therefore, unless maintenance occurs, this bridge will become structurally unsafe.

explanation.correct: A precisely parallels the original: (1) Cannot adapt/diversify under condition X → negative outcome. (2) Factor Y increases ability to adapt/diversify. (3) Subject lacks factor Y. (4) Therefore, unless conditions stay stable/favorable, subject faces negative outcome. The conditional structure (unless conditions remain stable) is key and present only in A.
explanation.A: See correct explanation above.
explanation.B: B lacks the conditional 'unless' — it concludes the building WILL collapse, not just at risk.
explanation.C: C weakens the conclusion to 'likely' rather than using the conditional structure.
explanation.D: D lacks the conditional stability qualifier and concludes definitively.
explanation.E: E is close — it has an 'unless maintenance occurs' qualifier — but the mechanism (corrosion/coating) doesn't involve the generative factor (diversity/diversification) in the same way.

questions.stimulus:
Nadia: Social media platforms should be required to verify the identity of all users, because anonymity enables harassment and the spread of disinformation. When people cannot be held accountable for their statements, they say things online they would never say in person.

Owen: Identity verification would have a chilling effect on legitimate political speech. In many countries, people who criticize their governments face real danger if identified. Anonymity has historically been essential to political dissent and the free exchange of ideas.
questions.question_stem: Nadia and Owen's discussion most directly concerns whether

choices.A: governments should have access to social media user data
choices.B: anonymity on social media is a net benefit or net harm to society
choices.C: social media platforms are responsible for moderating their content
choices.D: the harms of online harassment outweigh the benefits of free speech
choices.E: identity verification technology is sufficiently reliable for widespread use

explanation.correct: B captures the core disagreement. Nadia argues anonymity enables harm (harassment, disinformation). Owen argues anonymity enables essential good (political dissent, free expression). They are directly disagreeing about whether anonymity is a net positive or negative for society.
explanation.A: A is not discussed by either speaker.
explanation.B: See correct explanation above.
explanation.C: C is related but neither speaker directly addresses platform moderation responsibility.
explanation.D: D is close but too narrow — Owen's argument isn't just about free speech generally but specifically about political safety.
explanation.E: E is not discussed — the technical feasibility of verification isn't mentioned.

questions.stimulus:
Journalist: Investigative reporting exposes corruption, informs voters, and holds power accountable. Even when the reporting causes embarrassment to public officials, the democratic function of a free press justifies publication.

Public official: The press often publishes unverified allegations based on anonymous sources, causing irreparable damage to reputations before the facts are established. The current legal standards for defamation are insufficiently protective of individuals in public life.
questions.question_stem: The journalist and the public official would most likely disagree about which of the following?

choices.A: Whether investigative journalism ever exposes genuine corruption
choices.B: Whether the current legal framework adequately balances press freedom with individual protection
choices.C: Whether public officials are subject to greater scrutiny than private citizens
choices.D: Whether anonymous sources should ever be used in investigative reporting
choices.E: Whether democracy requires a free press

explanation.correct: B identifies the core legal disagreement. The journalist implies the current press freedom standards are justified. The official explicitly argues current defamation standards are 'insufficiently protective.' They are directly in conflict about whether the current legal balance is appropriate.
explanation.A: A is not at issue — the official doesn't deny that corruption is sometimes exposed.
explanation.B: See correct explanation above.
explanation.C: C is not directly addressed by either speaker.
explanation.D: D is close — the official criticizes anonymous sources — but the journalist doesn't specifically defend anonymous sourcing.
explanation.E: E — the journalist would agree democracy requires free press, and the official doesn't dispute this.

questions.stimulus:
Kenji: Electric vehicles are better for the environment than gasoline cars because they produce no direct emissions.

Leila: That's not quite right. Electric vehicles produce no tailpipe emissions, but the electricity used to charge them often comes from coal or natural gas power plants, which do produce emissions. The environmental benefit depends entirely on the regional energy grid.
questions.question_stem: Kenji and Leila disagree about

choices.A: whether electric vehicles are more expensive than gasoline cars
choices.B: whether reducing carbon emissions is an important environmental goal
choices.C: whether electric vehicles provide a straightforward environmental improvement over gasoline cars
choices.D: whether renewable energy should power all electric vehicle charging
choices.E: whether electric vehicles will eventually replace all gasoline cars

explanation.correct: C identifies the precise point of disagreement. Kenji says EVs are definitively better environmentally. Leila says the answer is 'not quite right' and depends on the energy grid — it's not straightforward. They disagree about the certainty and simplicity of EVs' environmental benefit.
explanation.A: A is not mentioned by either speaker.
explanation.B: B is not disputed — both implicitly value environmental improvement.
explanation.C: See correct explanation above.
explanation.D: D is not discussed by either speaker.
explanation.E: E is not addressed.

questions.stimulus:
Architect: The proposed glass tower is an aesthetically bold design that will enhance the city's skyline and signal its modernity.

Historic preservationist: A tower of that scale and material would overwhelm the surrounding 19th-century neighborhood, destroying the visual coherence that makes the area worth visiting.
questions.question_stem: The architect and the preservationist disagree about whether

choices.A: glass is a suitable material for tall buildings in urban environments
choices.B: the proposed tower's design is compatible with its intended surroundings
choices.C: modern architecture has value in cities with historic districts
choices.D: aesthetic considerations should outweigh other factors in urban planning
choices.E: the proposed tower will attract tourists to the area

explanation.correct: B captures the direct disagreement. The architect thinks the tower will enhance the skyline — implying compatibility with or improvement of the environment. The preservationist says it will overwhelm and destroy the visual coherence of the neighborhood. They specifically disagree about whether this design fits its context.
explanation.A: A is not addressed — neither disputes glass as a material generally.
explanation.B: See correct explanation above.
explanation.C: C is possible background disagreement but isn't what they actually discuss.
explanation.D: D is a higher-level normative question neither directly addresses.
explanation.E: E is not mentioned.

questions.stimulus:
Economist: The evidence clearly shows that free trade agreements increase total economic output for all participating countries. The gains from specialization and comparative advantage more than offset the disruption to specific industries.

Labor advocate: Even if total GDP increases, free trade agreements often concentrate gains among investors and corporate shareholders while displacing manufacturing workers who lack the skills or mobility to transition to the jobs that remain. Aggregate numbers can mask significant distributional harm.
questions.question_stem: The economist and the labor advocate most directly disagree about

choices.A: whether free trade agreements increase total GDP for participating countries
choices.B: whether specialization and comparative advantage are valid economic principles
choices.C: whether aggregate economic gains are a sufficient measure of a trade agreement's overall value
choices.D: whether displaced manufacturing workers are able to find comparable employment
choices.E: whether investors and shareholders benefit more from free trade than workers do

explanation.correct: C captures the core issue. The economist argues the total output gains justify free trade. The labor advocate concedes the GDP point ('even if total GDP increases') but argues this aggregate measure misses distributional harm. Their actual dispute is whether looking at aggregate output is sufficient to evaluate a trade agreement's worth.
explanation.A: A is not disputed — the labor advocate explicitly concedes total GDP may increase.
explanation.B: B is not disputed.
explanation.C: See correct explanation above.
explanation.D: D is implied by the labor advocate's position but isn't the central disagreement.
explanation.E: E is a factual claim implied but not the principal point of contention.

questions.stimulus:
Rasha: Early childhood education programs dramatically improve long-term outcomes including graduation rates, lifetime earnings, and reduced criminality. Every dollar invested in high-quality early childhood education saves multiple dollars in future social costs.

Marcus: Those studies were conducted on intensive, highly targeted programs with exceptionally qualified staff. Large-scale government early childhood programs typically cannot replicate those conditions. The cost savings projected from small studies rarely materialize at scale.
questions.question_stem: Rasha and Marcus disagree about whether

choices.A: early childhood education has demonstrated benefits in research settings
choices.B: high-quality early childhood education requires exceptionally qualified staff
choices.C: government programs can deliver early childhood education at the level of quality demonstrated in research studies
choices.D: the long-term social benefits of education programs justify their cost
choices.E: early childhood education should be funded by the government

explanation.correct: C identifies the exact point of contention. Rasha cites studies showing dramatic benefits. Marcus accepts the research findings (doesn't dispute A) but argues large-scale government programs can't replicate the conditions that produced those results. Their disagreement is specifically about whether government implementation can achieve the quality shown in research.
explanation.A: A is not in dispute — Marcus accepts the research findings.
explanation.B: B is implied by Marcus but not the central disagreement.
explanation.C: See correct explanation above.
explanation.D: D frames the issue in terms of cost justification, but the actual dispute is about whether the cost savings will materialize at scale.
explanation.E: E is a normative policy question neither speaker directly addresses.

questions.stimulus:
Dmitri: Our department's new time-tracking software is intrusive and damages employee morale. People feel surveilled, and good employees will leave for employers who trust them.

Sofia: The time-tracking data has already allowed us to identify inefficiencies that we've since eliminated, saving the department significant costs. Employee morale data from our last survey shows no change since implementation.
questions.question_stem: Dmitri and Sofia most directly disagree about

choices.A: whether employees value workplace privacy
choices.B: whether the time-tracking software has had a negative effect on employee morale
choices.C: whether saving costs justifies surveillance of employees
choices.D: whether good employees will leave companies that monitor their time
choices.E: whether time-tracking software is a reliable way to identify inefficiencies

explanation.correct: B is the clearest point of disagreement. Dmitri claims the software damages morale. Sofia cites survey data showing no change in morale. They are directly contradicting each other on this specific factual/empirical question.
explanation.A: A is not directly addressed — the speakers discuss morale effects, not abstract privacy values.
explanation.B: See correct explanation above.
explanation.C: C is a normative question neither fully addresses.
explanation.D: D is Dmitri's prediction — Sofia doesn't directly dispute this specific claim.
explanation.E: E — Sofia implies it works; Dmitri doesn't dispute its efficiency-finding function.

questions.stimulus:
Marine biologist: The decline of coral reefs is primarily driven by ocean temperature increases from climate change. Conservation efforts focused on local threats like water pollution and overfishing, while valuable, cannot prevent reef collapse if global emissions continue to rise.

Conservation director: While climate change is a long-term threat, local coral reefs that are protected from pollution and fishing pressure show significantly greater resilience to temperature stress. Local conservation provides reefs with the best chance of surviving the warming that is already occurring.
questions.question_stem: The marine biologist and the conservation director most directly disagree about

choices.A: whether climate change poses a threat to coral reefs
choices.B: whether coral reefs are currently in decline
choices.C: whether local conservation efforts can meaningfully improve coral reef survival outcomes
choices.D: whether ocean temperature increases are caused by human carbon emissions
choices.E: whether overfishing is a significant threat to marine ecosystems

explanation.correct: C pinpoints the disagreement. The biologist says local conservation 'cannot prevent reef collapse' if global emissions continue — implying local efforts have limited meaningful impact. The director says local conservation significantly improves reef resilience and survival. They directly disagree about whether local efforts can make a meaningful difference.
explanation.A: A is not in dispute — both acknowledge climate change threatens reefs.
explanation.B: B is not in dispute — both acknowledge declines.
explanation.C: See correct explanation above.
explanation.D: D is not addressed by either speaker.
explanation.E: E is mentioned briefly but not the central disagreement.

questions.stimulus:
Theo: Allowing patients to access their own medical records freely and immediately empowers them to make better healthcare decisions and fosters trust between patients and providers.

Dr. Park: Some medical findings require careful explanation from a physician before a patient sees them. A patient discovering a cancer diagnosis or ambiguous test result without context can cause severe psychological harm and lead to harmful decisions based on misinterpretation.
questions.question_stem: Theo and Dr. Park disagree about

choices.A: whether patients should ultimately have the right to access their medical records
choices.B: whether medical records are often difficult for patients to interpret
choices.C: whether immediate, unmediated patient access to all medical information is beneficial
choices.D: whether physicians should be required to explain test results to patients
choices.E: whether patient trust in healthcare providers is important

explanation.correct: C captures the specific disagreement. Theo argues immediate, free access empowers patients. Dr. Park argues some results, without physician mediation, can cause psychological harm and misinterpretation. They disagree specifically about whether immediate, unmediated access is net beneficial.
explanation.A: A is not clearly in dispute — Dr. Park may support access in principle but want mediation.
explanation.B: B is implied by Dr. Park but not directly stated by Theo.
explanation.C: See correct explanation above.
explanation.D: D is implied by Dr. Park but not the central question.
explanation.E: E — Theo explicitly values trust; Dr. Park doesn't dispute its importance.

questions.stimulus:
Sociologist: The rise of remote work has enabled urban professionals to relocate to rural areas, bringing economic activity and talent to underserved communities.

Urban planner: The influx of remote workers into rural areas drives up housing costs, displacing long-term residents who cannot afford the price increases. The economic benefits accrue primarily to landlords and newcomers, not to the communities that existed there before.
questions.question_stem: The sociologist and the urban planner most directly disagree about

choices.A: whether remote work has become more common among urban professionals
choices.B: whether rural communities benefit on balance from the migration of remote workers
choices.C: whether housing costs have risen in rural areas with influxes of remote workers
choices.D: whether urban professionals should be encouraged to relocate to rural areas
choices.E: whether economic inequality between urban and rural areas has increased

explanation.correct: B is the central dispute. The sociologist says the migration brings economic benefit to rural communities. The planner says it drives out existing residents and benefits primarily newcomers and landlords. They directly disagree about whether these communities are net beneficiaries.
explanation.A: A is background context not in dispute.
explanation.B: See correct explanation above.
explanation.C: C is not disputed — the planner asserts housing costs rise, and the sociologist doesn't deny this.
explanation.D: D is a normative question neither fully addresses.
explanation.E: E is not addressed by either speaker.

questions.stimulus: A wealthy benefactor offered to donate a large sum to the city's public library system under one condition: the library must name its main reading room after the benefactor's late mother. The city council accepted the donation and had the reading room renamed accordingly.
questions.question_stem: Which of the following principles, if valid, most helps to justify the city council's decision?

choices.A: Public institutions should not accept private donations that come with conditions attached
choices.B: When a private donor's conditions for a donation do not compromise the institution's core mission, it is appropriate to accept those conditions
choices.C: The naming of public spaces should always be decided through a public vote
choices.D: Honoring deceased individuals through public institutions is an acceptable form of civic recognition
choices.E: Financial considerations should never be the primary reason for changing the name of a public facility

explanation.correct: B most directly justifies the council's decision. The naming condition doesn't compromise the library's function or mission. B provides the principle that makes this acceptable: a naming condition that doesn't undermine the institution's purpose is a legitimate condition to accept.
explanation.A: A would condemn the decision, not justify it.
explanation.B: See correct explanation above.
explanation.C: C would require a different process than what occurred — it doesn't justify the council's decision.
explanation.D: D justifies naming things after deceased individuals in general but doesn't address the conditionality of the donation.
explanation.E: E potentially condemns the decision if the only reason was financial — it doesn't justify it.

questions.stimulus: A physician tells a patient that a proposed surgical procedure carries a 5 percent risk of serious complications. The patient, who understands the risk, consents to the procedure. During surgery, a complication occurs, resulting in harm to the patient.
questions.question_stem: Which of the following principles, if established, most directly supports the conclusion that the physician is not morally responsible for the harm?

choices.A: Medical professionals are responsible only for outcomes that result from their negligence or error, not from known risks the patient has accepted
choices.B: Patients who consent to medical procedures assume full responsibility for all outcomes, including those resulting from physician error
choices.C: Physicians are not responsible for any harm that occurs during surgery, since surgery is inherently risky
choices.D: Informed consent absolves a physician of any obligation to minimize surgical risk
choices.E: A physician's moral responsibility for a patient's harm is proportional to the probability of that harm occurring

explanation.correct: A directly supports the conclusion. The patient was informed of the risk and consented. The complication falls within the disclosed risk category, not physician negligence. A states that physicians are responsible only for negligence, not for realized known risks the patient accepted — which exactly covers this situation.
explanation.A: See correct explanation above.
explanation.B: B is too broad — it eliminates physician responsibility even for negligence, which goes beyond what is needed.
explanation.C: C is too sweeping — it would eliminate all surgical liability, including for negligence.
explanation.D: D is incorrect — consent doesn't mean physicians can be negligent.
explanation.E: E introduces proportionality that doesn't resolve the question of whether the physician is responsible at all.

questions.stimulus: A defense attorney knows that her client is guilty of the crime charged. Nevertheless, she mounts the most vigorous defense possible, arguing that the prosecution's evidence was obtained illegally and should be excluded.
questions.question_stem: Which of the following principles, if accepted, most strongly supports the attorney's conduct?

choices.A: Attorneys should be truthful in all representations they make to the court
choices.B: An attorney's duty is to represent a client's legal interests fully and zealously, within the bounds of the law, regardless of the client's guilt
choices.C: Guilty defendants do not deserve the same legal protections as innocent ones
choices.D: Evidence obtained illegally should always be excluded from criminal trials
choices.E: Legal professionals who knowingly help guilty clients escape justice are complicit in harm to society

explanation.correct: B is the principle that underlies the attorney's conduct. The defense attorney is doing exactly what B prescribes: representing her client's interests zealously and within the law (challenging illegal evidence collection is lawful). Her personal knowledge of guilt is irrelevant to her professional obligation.
explanation.A: A might seem in tension with the attorney's conduct — but the attorney isn't making false statements; she's making legal arguments about evidence admissibility.
explanation.B: See correct explanation above.
explanation.C: C would condemn the attorney's conduct, not support it.
explanation.D: D is a principle about evidence — it might support the specific argument made but not the overall conduct of defending a known-guilty client.
explanation.E: E would condemn the attorney's conduct.

questions.stimulus: A newspaper received a tip that a prominent CEO was secretly receiving payments from a competitor. The editors verified the information and published the story. The CEO suffered significant reputational damage and was forced to resign.
questions.question_stem: Which of the following principles most strongly justifies the newspaper's decision to publish?

choices.A: Individuals in positions of public trust have a reduced expectation of privacy with respect to conduct related to their public roles
choices.B: Journalists should protect the identity of confidential sources regardless of the consequences
choices.C: The reputational damage caused by accurate reporting is always justified by the public's right to know
choices.D: Newspapers should not publish stories that cause harm to specific individuals
choices.E: Corporate leaders should be judged by higher ethical standards than ordinary employees

explanation.correct: A directly justifies the decision. The CEO is a prominent public figure in a leadership role. The conduct (taking competitor payments) is directly related to that public role. A provides the principle that such figures have reduced privacy expectations for role-related conduct — supporting publication.
explanation.A: See correct explanation above.
explanation.B: B addresses source protection, not the publication decision.
explanation.C: C is too absolute — 'always justified' would cover even marginal public interest, which is overbroad.
explanation.D: D would condemn the decision.
explanation.E: E concerns ethical standards, not the press's right to publish.

questions.stimulus: A city requires all food cart vendors to obtain a $2,000 annual license. Large restaurant chains operating in the same city are exempt from this licensing requirement because they are already regulated under a separate commercial restaurant framework. A food cart vendor argues that the dual system is unfair.
questions.question_stem: Which of the following principles, if true, most supports the vendor's claim of unfairness?

choices.A: Regulatory frameworks should be designed to minimize paperwork burdens on small businesses
choices.B: Businesses that compete in the same market and pose similar risks to consumers should be subject to equivalent regulatory burdens
choices.C: Large corporations should be required to pay more in regulatory fees than small businesses because they have greater resources
choices.D: Licensing requirements should be proportional to the potential harm a business poses to the public
choices.E: Government should not regulate businesses that are already subject to any form of private-sector oversight

explanation.correct: B is the principle that directly supports the vendor's fairness claim. The food carts and restaurants compete in the same food service market. If they pose similar consumer risks, they should face similar regulatory burdens. The fact that restaurants are regulated under a different framework doesn't justify imposing heavier licensing costs on carts that compete directly with them.
explanation.A: A is relevant but too vague — it doesn't specifically address competitive fairness.
explanation.B: See correct explanation above.
explanation.C: C argues in the opposite direction from what the vendor needs — it would support HEAVIER burdens on chains.
explanation.D: D might actually support the current system if food carts are deemed riskier — it doesn't help the vendor.
explanation.E: E is about private-sector oversight and doesn't address the vendor's situation.

questions.stimulus: During a hostage negotiation, a police officer promises a hostage-taker immunity from prosecution in exchange for releasing the hostages unharmed. The hostage-taker complies and releases all hostages. The district attorney subsequently decides to prosecute anyway, arguing that the officer had no authority to offer immunity.
questions.question_stem: Which of the following principles, if valid, would most support criticizing the district attorney's decision to prosecute?

choices.A: Law enforcement officers should always consult with prosecutors before making promises to suspects
choices.B: Individuals who rely in good faith on official assurances should not be penalized when those assurances are repudiated by other authorities
choices.C: Negotiated agreements between law enforcement and criminals should always be honored regardless of the circumstances
choices.D: The safety of hostages outweighs all other considerations in a hostage situation
choices.E: Prosecutorial decisions should be independent of law enforcement conduct during negotiations

explanation.correct: B is the principle that most supports criticizing the prosecution. The hostage-taker complied — he released the hostages based on the officer's promise. Prosecuting someone who relied in good faith on an official promise violates the principle of promissory fairness and undermines the efficacy of future negotiations.
explanation.A: A criticizes the officer, not the DA's prosecution decision.
explanation.B: See correct explanation above.
explanation.C: C is too absolute — some negotiated agreements might need to be revisited.
explanation.D: D addresses the emergency situation, not the subsequent prosecution decision.
explanation.E: E would support the DA's decision, not criticize it.

questions.stimulus: A pharmaceutical company has developed a drug that cures a rare disease affecting only about 500 people worldwide. The drug is extraordinarily expensive to produce. The company priced the drug at $300,000 per year, which makes it unaffordable for most patients.
questions.question_stem: Which of the following principles most directly supports the view that the company's pricing is ethically problematic?

choices.A: Companies that develop life-saving treatments have a duty not to profit from human suffering
choices.B: When a medical treatment is the only available cure for a condition, the developer should not price it beyond the reach of those who need it
choices.C: Pharmaceutical pricing should always be regulated by government authorities
choices.D: Rare disease treatments should be subsidized by the government rather than sold at market prices
choices.E: Companies have an obligation to invest their profits in further medical research

explanation.correct: B directly addresses this situation: a unique cure priced beyond patient reach. B states that when something is the only available cure, it shouldn't be priced out of reach of patients who need it — which is exactly the criticism of $300,000/year for a disease affecting 500 people.
explanation.A: A is too broad — it prohibits all profit from life-saving drugs, not just monopoly pricing.
explanation.B: See correct explanation above.
explanation.C: C prescribes a regulatory mechanism, not an ethical standard.
explanation.D: D proposes a policy solution rather than an ethical principle about the company's pricing behavior.
explanation.E: E addresses research reinvestment, not pricing ethics.
